of the Village of Woodbury Zoning Code entitled “Dwelling Unit for an additional family member”. The
Pucciarelli property is located at 4 Dartmouth Road., Highland Mills, NY and is further designated on the
Village of Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 245 Block 1 Lot 115. . A public hearing was held on July 23,
2014, notice of which was published in the Times Herald Record and read by the Chairperson at the
hearing.

would substantially serve the public’s convenience, safety and welfare and will not be detrimental to the
neighborhood or residences thereof and will not be otherwise detrimental to the public convenience or
welfare: now, therefore, it is resolved that the Board finds that the applicant has sustained its burden of
proof and that a Temporary Special Permit be issued to Joseph Pucciarelli to allow for the completion of
the kitchen and installation of a stove in the lower level of the premises known as 4 Dartmouth Road,
Highland Mills, NY pursuant to Section 310-35 of the Village of Woodbury Zoning Code.
Motion was offered by K Abrams, Seconded by A Giacomazza, to adopt the decision as read,
accepting the application (full decision is on file in the office of the Village Clerk). Chairman Ferrarelli
conducted a roll call of the Board which resulted in the motion being:

name: Baker- The Board reviewed the application by Frank Baker and Kim Baker for area variances
raw_block:
from Section 310-6 and Section 310-7 of the Village of Woodbury Zoning Code for their property located
at 24 Janice Drive, Highland Mills, NY which is known and designated on the Village of Woodbury Tax
Map as Section 204 Block 1m Lot 88. The applicant requests to be permitted to construct an attached
garage which would leave a side yard o 26 feet whereas the Village of Woodbury Zoning Code requires a
30 foot side yard in the R2A District. A public hearing was held on July 23, 2014, notice of which was
published in the Times Herald Record and read by the Chairperson at the hearing.
The Board hereby finds that the applicant has sustained its burden of proof as required
by both the Village Law of the State of New York and the Village of Woodbury Zoning Code with respect
to its application for area variances to allow for the construction of an attached garage at its premises
located at 24 Janice Drive, Highland Mills, NY. The Board found that the proposed area variances would
not be detrimental to the health, safety and welfare of the neighborhood of the applicant’s property
and hereby grants the applicant’s request to allow for the construction of a n attached garage of 30 feet
6 % inches by 30 feet which would leave a side yard of 26 feet whereas the Village of Woodbury Zoning
Code requires a side yard of 30 feet in this zone. The Board based its decision on, among other things,
the fact that the addition was being placed on a flat location in the vicinity of an already existing
driveway on a piece of property that is well hidden from passers by.
Motion was offered by K Abrams, Seconded by K. Ungerer, to adopt the decision as read,
accepting the application (full decision is on file in the office of the Village Clerk). Chairman Ferrarelli
conducted a roll call of the Board which resulted in the motion being:
ADOPTED - AYES 4 Ferrarelli, Abrams, Giacomazza, Ungerer
NOES 0

; Chairman Ferrarelli abstained from this application for reasons known to the board
‘Zoning Board Member Kevin Abrams read the decision prepared by Attorney Felicissima, as
follows:
The Board hereby finds that the applicant has sustained its burden of proof as required by both
the Village Law of the State of New York and the Village of Woodbury Zoning Code with respect to its
application for an area variance from Section 310-25(A) of the Village of Woodbury Zoning Code to allow
for the installation of an in-ground pool at the applicant's property located at 1 Carlisle Court, Highland
Mills, New York. The board found that the proposed are variance would not be detrimental to the
health, safety and welfare of the neighborhood and hereby grants the applicant's request for an area
variance to allow for installation of the in the ground pool as set forth on the Plot Plan attached to the
application. The board based this decision on, among other things, the unique topography at the
premises, the fact that there are no public comment that the public hearing, the fact that the Brigadoon
Homeowner's Association has already passed upon it and has granted the necessary approval to the
applicant subject to this board’s and all municipal approvals etc. The area variance is contingent upon
the applicant’s compliance with any and all of the rules and regulations of the Brigadoon and Highland
mills Homeowners” Association with respect to the pool and fence as well as full compliance with the
Village of Woodbury fence requirement for in-ground pools.

The Board hereby finds that the applicant has sustained its burden of proof as required by both
the Village Law of the State of New York and the Village of Woodbury Zoning Code with respect to its
application for area variances from Section and 310-7 (Bulk Regulations) and 310-6(B) of the Village of
Woodbury Zoning Code for side yard setbacks and a front yard setback. The Board hereby grants the
applicant’s request for a front yard setback to allow for a 16.8 foot front yard setback whereas the
Village of Woodbury Zoning Code Bulk Regulations requires a 40 foot setback. Additionally, the Board
grants the applicants request for side yard setbacks for the bulk regulations of the Village of Woodbury
Zoning Code to allow for 18 foot side yard setbacks on both sides of the home whereas the Village of
Woodbury Zoning Code calls for 30 foot setbacks on to each side and a total of 60 feet combined. These
variances, however, are subject to the applicant’s full compliance with any and all requirements of the
Village of Woodbury Building Department and conditions imposed by the Village of Woodbury Building
Inspector.

conducted a roll call of the Board which resulted in the motion being:

The Board hereby finds that the applicant has sustained its burden of proof as required by both
the Village Law of the state of New York and the Village of Woodbury Zoning Code with respect to its
application for an area variance to allow for the construction of an accessory building (shedO in a side
yard at their property known and designated as 96 Woodward Terrace, central Valley, New York
whereas the Woodbury Zoning Code precludes any accessory building from being placed in a side yard.
The Board based its decision on, among other things, the unique and sloping nature of the property and
the inability of the homeowner to place the proposed accessory building in the rear yard due to the
existence of septic and gas lines. Accordingly, the Board grants the applicants’ request for a variance
from Sections 310-6 and 310-11(C) of the Village of Woodbury Zoning Code to allow for the installation
of an accessory building (shed) set forth on the plan submitted to the building department together with
the applicant’s application.

label: B
name: Exclusive Motor Sports
raw_block:
A continuation of the public hearing was held for area variances to permit the construction of a new
mixed use structure, proposing 9,300 square feet of retail space and 16 apartments. The applicant
proposes parking in the front yard, within 50 feet ofa State Highway, whereas the Zoning Code requires
that the initial 50 feet off a State Highway be unoccupied and prohibits parking in a required front yard
in any district under Section 310-40. Additionally, the HB District requires a minimum of 6,000 square
feet per residential unit, and 10,000 square feet for retail establishments. Section 310-7 also permits
maximum lot coverage of 75% and the applicant proposes lot coverage of 100%, Furthermore, pursuant
to Section 310-7, the maximum building height is two stories, whereas the application is proposing three
stories. Said property is located in the HB Zoning District at 279 Route 32 in Central Valley and is known
on the Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 230, Block 4, and Lot 4.2.
Alan Lipman, the attorney representing Exclusive Motor Sports was there on behalf of the
owner to discuss the changes to the site plan and the changes that will be made between now and the
last time they were before the planning board. He stated that the zone was changed to reflect the
proper zoning to build a structure like this one. The zoning district is now In the HP district, and they
want to help encourage growth within the district. Attorney Lipman said they will be seeking two
separate variances. One being the lot area of the parcel the second being off street parking on the side
yard, 50 feet off the highway which would be allowed since it is a non-conforming use of the land stated
in section 310-43.2B of the Zoning Code. Attorney Lipman also stated that they will not make an

label: C
name: Mortimer
raw_block:
A public hearing was held for an interpretation by the Board of Section 310-8 of the Village of
Woodbury Zoning Code and Section 310-12 (A) (2) of the Village of Woodbury Zoning Code as it applies
to the property. In the alternative, applicant seeks area variances from Section 310-8 and 310-12(A) of
the Village of Woodbury Zoning Code to allow for the subdivision of its property known and designated
as Section 233 Block 1 Lot 7.31. The applicant proposed a subdivision for property which is bordered by
the New York State Thruway and Route 6. Said property is located in the R3A Zoning District at the
Southeast intersection of I-87 (New York State Thruway} and US Route 6 and is known on the Village of
Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 233 Block 1 Lot 7.31
James G. Sweeney, Attorney representing the interests of the three Mortimer brothers, Jay,
David and Averell, spoke regarding this Application. The Mortimer’s, who are the grandchildren of
Governor Averell Harriman and the children of his daughter, Kathleen Harriman Mortimer, are desirous
of subdividing some of the remaining lands of the Harriman estate for purposes of conveying 290.9 acres
to the Open Space Institute, which, in turn will convey the lands to the Palisades Interstate Park
Commission for passive open space use. Lots No. 1, will ultimately become part of the Palisades
Interstate Park Commission’s holdings which is in line with the Harriman family’s long standing goals of
preserving these lands from development and to commit them to the public domain through the PIPC.
The balance (Lot No.2) will remain in the Mortimer’s ownership. All lots would be restricted from any
development by way of recorded instrument and a map not. The proposed subdivision is now before the
planning Board for review and approval.

label: D
name: Gross/ Yesterday Village
raw_block:
A Public Hearing was held for a use variance to permit a pawn shop (retail) business in the CR
Zone. Pursuant to Section 310-6(a) & 310-7, bulk regulations, a retail business is not a permitted use in
the CR Zoning District. The variance is requested based on a denial of a building permit for proposed
use. Said property is located at 877 Route 32 in Highland Mills and is known on the Village of Woodbury
Tax Map as Section 207 Block 1 Lot 1.15.
Bryan Gross, was there on behalf of his father Anthony Gross to discuss their business that has
already been there since the 1940's. They want to bring their business into the 21" century but keep
the same style of business of buying, and selling antiques, and putting things on loan for people. The
only thing that has changed is that they have enhanced signage. The pawn shop signs are up in front of
the business, where there were no pawn shop signs before. Attorney Felicissimo, inquired about a
special permit, and if so it should be done at the planning board level. K. Ungerer asked if there was any
construction going on. Bryan Gross stated that there was no construction going on and that the buying
and selling of antiques has gone down 20% since the 1990's, 85% of their store will be antiques, the
other 15% will be pawn. Chairman Ferrarelli informed Bryan Gross that he should be applying for a use
variance or an Interpretation, and he should get that before the board discusses this matter further.
Motion was offered by K. Abrams, seconded by A. Giacomazza, to carry over the public hearing
to the December 10, 2014 meeting. Chairman Ferrarelli conducted a roll call of the Board which resulted
in the motion being:
ADOPTED AYES 4 Ferrarelli, Abrams, Giacomazza, Ungerer
NOES 0

southeast intersection of I-87 (New York State Thruway} and US Route 6 and is known on the Village of
Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 233 Block 1 Lot 7.31.
Attorney James Sweeney, is here on behalf of the Mortimer family. The Mortimer family is
attempting to carve out a section that stems down into Tuxedo. Attorney Sweeney would like to
reiterate, what he has said at past public hearings, which is: to subdivide some of the remaining lands of
the Harriman estate for purposes of conveying 290.9 acres to the Open Space Institute, which, in turn will
convey the lands to the Palisades Interstate Park Commission for passive open space use. Lots No. 1,
shown on the plat submitted, will ultimately become part of the PIPC’s holdings which is in line with the
Harriman family’s long standing goals of preserving these lands fram development and to commit them
to the public domain through the PIPC. The balanced Lot No. 2 will remain in the Mortimer’s’ ownership.
More importantly, the purpose of a street or road frontage requirement for new lots is to afford
adequate access to the structures on the new lots for emergency service vehicles, e.g., fire, police and
ambulance vehicles, and heavy duty service vehicles such as fuel delivery and garbage trucks for access
to the buildings to be built on the lots. This purpose can be gleaned from the planning Board’s own
subdivision regulations g

Enforcement Officer pursuant to Section 310-49 (C13), that a pawn shop is not considered an accessory
use (retail) which would not be permitted within the CR Zoning District pursuant to Section 310-7 bulk
regulation table. Said property is located in the CR Zoning District at 877 Route 32 in Highland Mills and
is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax Map as Section 207 Block 1 Lot 1.15.
Attorney Paul Webber, is assisting Mr. Gross with his application, both the applicant and the
attorney approached the podium. Attorney Webber stated that he researched “pawn shops” and stated
that years ago it was called a “Trading Post”. Chairman Ferrarelli, asked the applicant to explain to him
what his interpretation of a pawn shop is. Mr. Gross, stated that a pawnbroker is an individual or
business that offers secured loans to people, with items of personal property used as collateral. If an
item is pawned for a Joan, within a certain contractual period of time the pawn-er may redeem it for the
amount of the loan plus some agreed-upon amount for interest. The amount of time, and rate of
interest, is governed by law or by the pawnbroker's policies, If the loan is not paid (or extended, if
applicable) within the time period, the pawned item will be offered for sale by the pawnbroker. Unlike
other lenders, the pawnbroker does not report the defaulted loan on the customer's credit report, since
the pawnbroker has physical possession of the item and may recoup the loan value through outright
sale of the item. The pawnbroker also sells items that have been sold outright to them by customers.
Chairman Ferrarelli, asked the interest rate to pawn an item and if it was in the contract? If the interest
rate was the same amount for everyone? And if it was posted somewhere within the store. K. Abrams
wanted to know if there was a set rate. Mr. Gross, stated that it is a set rate of 4.99%, it was not po

Public Hearing for an interpretation pursuant to Section 310-49C (3). Where-as applicant
proposes to modify and create an additional wetland area within an existing agricultural use (Ace Farm)
and seeks an interpretation as to whether the additional wetland area is considered a separate use or
falls within the existing agricultural use. Said property is located in the R2A Zoning District at 398 County
Route 105 in Highland Mills and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 213, Block 1,
Lot 29.1.
Joseph Scarmato, Esq, approached the podium and explained to the board that the Village of
Woodbury Planning board has, with the recommendation of the Village Building Inspector, has referred
the Ace Farm Landscaping project to the Zoning Board for consideration and resolution. The proposed
project will expand an existing wetland on the Ace Farm site to yi9eld almost five acres of additional
wetlands that will be protected by Federal regulation under the jurisdiction of the U.A, Army Corps of
Engineers (USACOE). The project has the approval of the USACOE and support of the New York State
Department of Environmental Conservation (NYSDEC). The project consists of the grading and wetland
planting of a small portion of the Ace Farm site. No soils will be imported or exported from the site.
Erasion control measure will be implemented during the projects construction. The expanded wetlands
will be maintained until stabilized. Subsequently, there will be no activity within the wetland area as its
sole function is to provide the environmental benefits that wetlands are recognized to provide, The
Village of Woodbury Building inspector has advised that this “use” of the land as a wetlands may differ
from its zoned “Agricultural” use and has suggested that the ZBA consider this matter. From the
perspective of the applicant the definition of the term “use”, the expanded wetlands is not a “use” at all
as it entails no future activity of any kind. Alternatively, the projec

label: E
name: Rabinowitz
raw_block:
Public Hearing for an interpretation pursuant to Section 310-49C (3). The applicant had been
denied a Building Permit to construct a large addition to an existing single family dwelling and the
applicant requests an interpretation as to whether this addition would fall under the definition of a
Single Family dwelling. Said property is located in the R2A Zoning District at 80 Seven Springs Road in
Highland Mills and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 213, Block 1, Lot 18.41.
Mr. John Queenan a consultant for the project stated that the Rabinowitz family is a very warm
and bonded family originating from and currently residing in Brooklyn, NY. They often retreat in the
Catskills during the summer months; typically the whole family which is now three generations, as the
family has grown over the years they have been looking to create their private family owned resort land
where the entire family including Mr. and Mrs. Rabinowitz, their children, and the even the children of
their married off children could retreat in a pleasurable atmosphere with convenience and comfort.

Building Department of January 29, 2015, comes before this Board as an appeal of a determination
made by Gary Thomasberger, the Village of Woodbury Code Enforcement Officer (a/k/a “Building
Inspector”) contained in his letter dated December 1, 2014, The Zoning Board of Appeals issued its
original Decision on this application on June 10, 2015. Subsequently, Exclusive Motor-Sports, LLC, the
owner of the property known on the Village tax maps as Section 230, Block 4, Lot 4.2, challenged that
Decision by way of an Article 78 proceeding. On October 28, 2015, the Supreme Court, Orange County,
issued a decision ordering that the Zoning Board’s June 10, 2015 decision be annulled and vacated, and
remanded the matter back to the Board “for further hearings, evidence and argument, findings and a
new decision not inconsistent with the decision of the court.”
After many facts, findings, testimonials, and deliberations the Zoning Board of Appeals came to
the following decision.
In conclusion, it is hereby determined that the proposed alteration of the use of the front yard
area (setback 50-feet from Route 32) on the Exclusive Motor-Sports Property from its historical use for
the display of vehicles for sale to commercial/retail and residential parking is qualitatively dissimilar, and
materially alters the essence of the existing nonconforming use. The determination of the Building
Inspector concerning this matter is annulled and the determination on the quarry at issue shall be
consistent with this decision.

a parcel of land that exceeds 11 acres. The proposed hotel would be located on less than three acres of
the site. The remaining lands would not be disturbed and will be maintained in their existing condition
as open space. The potential for off-site impacts on traffic is addressed by the unique circumstances of
Estrada Road, which was constructed by New York State as a State Highway in 1912, and used as State
Highway 6, until the new Route 6 highway was constructed in 1970. Although New York State
transferred maintenance responsibilities to the Town of Woodbury by Order of the Department of
Transportation, the physical roadway continues with the same form and function it has while operating
as Route 6. That roadway is physically capable of handling the anticipated traffic from the hotel, as it
serves a variety of existing commercial uses, including a used car business recently approved by the

application for area variances to allow for the installation and the construction of an 8 foot tall solid
vinyl fencing and 8 foot tall stockade wooden fencing around the perimeter of its property as set forth

two openings or gates so that emergency vehicles can access the property as well as making sure that in
the vicinity of the driveway, the fencing is not a straight fence but will go up approximately 10 feet along
the driveway and then back down as to avoid a straight fencing and enclosing of the driveway.

requirements of the Village of Woodbury Building Department concerning fencing etc. The Board found

label: A
name: Ader~
raw_block:
Review decision for an area variance to permit the installation of a 6 ft. high solid vinyl fence Ina
side yard. Property located at 12 Pheasant Run, HM SBL 247-2-6,
A public hearing was held on October 12, 2016, notice of which was published in the Times
Herald Record and read by the Chairman at the hearing.
The Board hereby finds that the applicant has sustained its burden of proof as required by both
the Village Law of the State of New York and the Village of Woodbury Zoning Code with respect to its
application for area variance to install a 6 foot tall mostly solid viny! fence as set forth in the proposed
plan attached to the application submitted to the Village of Woodbury Building Department and grants
the apoplicant’s request for the above referenced area variances. The decision was based on, among
other things, the fact that the applicant is in need of creating privacy and screening from various
members of the community who are gathering on the street with the apparent intent to view members
of his family. Moreover, the applicant indicated that the suggested alternative of planting a row of trees
in the vicinity of the proposed fence would be extremely expensive and take several years to mature.
Additionally, the Board acknowledged that the applicant was simply attempting to screen one area of
his yard and was not attempting to enclose the entire property with fencing. The applicant was
requesting to install fencing only in the area where it would be required to create the privacy. The
Board based its decision on, among other things, the fact that the alternative of installation of trees
would be time consuming and cost prohibitive. Accordingly, the applicant’s request for a variance from
Section 146-5(A)(B) and 146-7 is hereby granted and the applicant is permitted to install the above
described fence.
Motion was offered by K. Abrams, Seconded by A. Giacomazza, to adopt the decision as read,
accepting the application (full decision is on file in the office

label: B
name: Terach/Schwimmer
raw_block:
Review decision for an area variance to permit the installation of a 9 ft. high fence in a rear
yard. Property is located at 219 Seven Springs Rd, HM SBL 213-1- 60.
A public hearing was held on October 12, 2016, notice of which was published in the
Times Herald Record and read by the Chairman at the hearing.
The Board hereby finds that the applicant has not sustained its burden of proof as required by
both the Village Law of the State of New York and the Village of Woodbury Zoning Code with respect to
its application for an area variance to install a 9 foot tall solid fence around the poal in the back yard of
its property located at 219 Seven Springs Rd., Highland Mills, New York and denies the applicant’s
request for the above referenced area variance. The decision was based on, among other things, the
fact that the applicant did not prove that they attempted to consider any other means of obtaining the
privacy they indicate is required. The applicant responded to the Board that they did not consider
interior or exterior window shades, a shorter fence, temporary tarps or tall tree plantings before
requesting the subject variance. Additionally, the variance is substantial and various members of the
public raised safety issues with there being a total block out of the pool area from individuals within the
home who should be monitoring the swim. Additionally, various Board Members were concerned that
the installation of a 9’ fence along the entire perimeter of the proposed swimming pool would create a
fortress-like surrounding and that there were simply other methods of achieving privacy that the
applicant did not attempt to show the Board that they considered. The Board found that the proposed
area variance would have more of a negative impact than positive at the applicant’s property located at
219 Seven Springs Rd., Highland Mills, New York.
Motion was offered by J. DeVenuto, Seconded by K. Ungerer, to adopt the decision as read,
denying the application (full decis

name: Zucker ~
raw_block:
Review decision for an area variance for an addition to the existing single family dwelling leaving
a side yard setback of 10ft and. Special Permit to permit the use their existing single family for an
additional family member (mother/daughter}, Property is located in the RIA Zoning District at 22 Cindy
Lane, HM SBL 221-4-9.
The Board hereby finds, with respect to the applicant's request for the Special Permit for use of
the premises for an additional family member and the requested setback requirement to allow for a 10
ft. side yard setback whereas the Village of Woodbury Zoning Code requires a 30 ft. side yard setback in
the RIA Zoning District, that the applicant has sustained its burden of proof and hereby grants both
requests. The Board acknowledges the condition of Section 310-35 of the Village of Woodbury Zoning
Code which requires that a single family dwelling may be expanded up to 25% to a maximum of 1,000
square feet in order to accommodate the new unit. The Board also takes into consideration all of the
elements of the Village of Woodbury Zoning Code and the Village Law of the State of New York before
granting same. Said relief is granted subject to the following conditions: The Board advised the applicant
during the course of the public hearing that when and if ever the need for the dwelling unit for the
additional family member is no longer necessary, said kitchen shall be removed. The applicant and the
inhabitant upon transfer of the home or sale of the property, the Special Permit for the additional
family member shall cease and any new owner must come before the Board to request renewal of
same if said will be required. The granting of the Special Permit is also subject to an onsite
inspection by the administrator of the Building Department on the anniversary date of this Special
Permit to determine that there has been full compliance with the terms and conditions herein and
to determine if, in fact, the premises is still being used for an additional

label: A
name: Brown
raw_block:
Continuation of Public Hearing for an area variance to permit the construction of two additions
one located in the rear yard leaving a 10ft setback and one located in the side yard leaving a 12 ft.
setback. Whereas pursuant to Section 310-6B and 310-7 schedule of District regulations, the rear yard
Page 1 – Zoning Board of Appeals Minutes - May 10, 2017
setback required is 50ft and the side yard setback required is 30ft. Said property is located in the R2A
Zoning District at 4 First Avenue in Highland Mills and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax maps as
Section 214 Block 6 Lot 4.
Mr. Yoel Elkstein approached the podium and stated he is there on behalf of the Brown family.
The Brown family’s primary residence is in Brooklyn, however they use this property as a seasonal/
summer home which they use on some weekends and holidays. The house was renovated a few years
ago, and was in a run-down position prior to the renovation. The dining room is very small as well as the
house not having any room for the kids to play. The family needs a succa room for the succa holiday
therefore the family is requesting a variance to add on to the back of the house, as well as adjacent to
the dining room, add on to the side of the house as well to give the children more room to play.
Chairman Ferrarelli, asked when the addition is added will the family still use it as a seasonal home? Mr.
Elkstein said yes.
Chairman Ferrarelli, then inquired about the septic, and Mr. Elkstein stated that it is on the left
side of the house all the way down to the end of the property. Chairman Ferrarelli, then inquired about
basements, and was answered with a no.

label: C
name: Central Valley Properties
raw_block:
Continuation of Public Hearing for an area variance to permit the construction of a mixed use
building containing commercial retail, office, restaurant as well as 8 residential apartments. In
accordance with 310-6F and 310-7(district regulations) a minimum lot area of 78,000 square feet is
required, whereas the provided lot area has 64,248 square feet. Furthermore, due to an inconsistency
within the code, an area variance or interpretation is requested for proposed building with a front yard
setback of 14.9 feet. Section 310-12B require all properties with frontage on a state highway (Rt 32)
have a depth of 50 feet unoccupied and open to the sky and Section 310-7(b) permits a lesser front yard
setback requirement. Said property is located in the HB Zoning District at 252-260 Route 32 in Central
Valley and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax maps as Section 230 Block 7 Lot 17, 18 & 19.
Mr. Steve Esposito stated The Applicant is requesting relief from Section 310-6.F which requires
"where more than one principle use occupies a lot, said lot shall be large enough to satisfy minimum lot
area or density requirements of each use." As noted below in the 5 way test, it is the opinion of the
Applicant that relief from this provision of the Code for the HB Zone is consistent with the Village of
Woodbury Comprehensive Plan adopted in 2011. Granting the request would not change the
appearance or scale of the project.
Application before the Planning Board has defined three (3) potential uses (retail, office and
restaurant) on the first floor each of which require 10,000 square feet (30,000 sf) and eight (8)
apartments each of which require 6,000 sf (48,000 sf) for a total minimum lot area of 78,000 sf.

label: A
name: Kumar
raw_block:
Public hearing for an area variance to permit the construction of a new single family dwelling on a lot
having no frontage on a public street. Pursuant to Section 310-12(A)2 of Village Code and 280A of Town Law,
building lots must have a minimum frontage of 100 feet. Said vacant property is located in the R2A Zoning District
on Campbell Avenue (private road) in Highland Mills and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax maps as Section
216 Block 7 Lots 2, 3, 13 & 14.
Mr. John Queenan the applicants engineer was at the meeting to represent his client. He stated he is in
front of the ZBA to obtain a permit for the construction of a new single family dwelling on a lot having no frontage
on a public street. This property is the second property off Bakertown Road on Campbell Avenue in Highland Mills.
The property consists of four different parcels each being 10,000 square feet a piece. They want to combine all
four lots. The property is served by well and septic.
Chairwoman Ungerer, asked what the process to combine lots usually is. Mr. Queenan stated the
applicant goes to the building department, sometimes the Planning Board, receives lot consolidation and files it
with the county. Chairwoman Ungerer, then asked if there is a road maintenance agreement? Mr. Queenan
stated per his knowledge and his clients (the owners of the property) there is no such agreement. At this point the
public (other homeowners from Campbell Avenue) interjected stating there is an agreement. Chairwoman
Ungerer, asked how many people will live in this house, if it is built. Mr. Queenan then stated it is being built as a
single family. Chairwoman Ungerer, then asked how many houses are on Campbell Avenue. The public again
interjected with the answer seven.

label: E
name: Zucker
raw_block:
Public Hearing for a Special Permit to permit the use their existing single family for an additional family
member (mother/daughier) pursuant to Section 310-36 of the Village Code. Said property is located in the
RIA Zoning District at 22 Cindy Lane in Highland Mills and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax maps
as Section 221 Block 4 Lot 9.
Being that both applications are by the same applicant, Mr. Zucker spoke about both the area variance
and the special permit request in unison.
The applicant approached the podium, Chairman Ferrarelli, stated that the applicant was in front of the
ZBA prior to this meeting. He then stated that questions were brought up previously that the applicant was
given the special permit for a mother daughter permit in the past. Chairman Ferrarelli, stated that it did in
fact exist and they were given permission to have a mother/daughter on February 27, 1994. Chairman
Ferrarelli, then stated that they basically went over everything the last time the applicant was in front of
them. Attorney Felicissimo stated that the only reason they are still here was because there was a
discrepancy in the public hearing notice, and that needed fo be fixed before they could close the hearing.
Mr. Randy Vite stated he didn’t have a problem with the mother daughter, however he has a problem
with the staircase being so close to his property. He then went on to say that he has not seen a plan on it,
and he thinks there are other alternatives. He suggested a spiral staircase, he wants to sell his house and
he doesn’t want the house to look like an apartment building.

label: F
name: Weinstock/SG Enterprises
raw_block:
Public Hearing for an area variance to permit the demolition of an existing 2 family seasonal
dwelling and the construction of a new larger single family seasonal dwelling. Proposed construction will
leave a front yard of 33.62 feet and a rear yard of 31.28 feet, whereas, pursuant to the schedule of District
Regulations Sections 310-6B & 310-7, a 40 foot front yard and 50 foot rear yard is required. Furthermore,
proposed dwelling has no frontage on a public road, whereas pursuant to Section 310-8, dwelling must
have frontage on a State, County or Village road. Said property is located in the R2A Zoning District on a
private road at 6 Second Street in Highland Mills and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax maps as
Section 214 Block 7 Lot 6.
Mr. Yoel Weinstein representing the Weinstock’s. Mr. Weinstein stated that the Weinstocks have
their temporary residence in Brooklyn, however they are looking to remodel this house and make it their
seasonal house. The Building Inspector informed the applicant that the structure was unsafe and ordered
that it should be demolished and rebuilt. Because of the size of the house the applicant wants fo build, the
Weinstock’s were informed that they needed to request an area variance.
Chairman Ferrarelli, asked if any part of the old house or the foundation would be used? Applicant
representative stated no, if will most likely have to be replaced. Chairman Ferrarelli, then asked who was
constructing the new house? Mr. Weinstein stated they have not hired anyone yet.

label: A
name: Kolodzinski
raw_block:
Review decision for area variance to permit the installation of a 10 x 14 detached shed
(accessory building) partially located within the front and side yard. Said property is located in the R1A
Zoning District at 73 Edgewood Dr CV SBL 228-1-1.3.
The board hereby finds that the applicant has sustained its burden of proof as required by both
the Village Law of the State of New York and the Village of Woodbury Zoning code with respect to its
application for an area variance from Section 310-11C of the Village of Woodbury Zoning Code which
would allow the placement of the pre-constructed shed partially in the side yard and front yard at the
applicant’s property located at 73 Edgewood Drive., Central Valley, NY and grants the applicant’s
request. The decision is subject to and contingent upon the applicant’s compliance with all of the other
existing Village of Woodbury Zoning Code requirements and compliance with the Village of Woodbury
Building Departments requirements concerning permits etc. Additionally, this variance is contingent
upon the property owner leaving a 6’ setback from the side property line. The Board found that the
proposed area variance would not be detrimental to the health, safety and welfare of the neighborhood
for the applicant’s property location at 73 Edgewood Drive Central Valley, New York.
Motion was offered by K. Abrams, Seconded by A. Giacomazza, to adopt the decision as read,
accepting the application (full decision is on file in the office of the Village Clerk). K. Abrams, conducted
a roll call of the Board which resulted in the motion being:
ADOPTED AYES 4 Abrams, Giacomazza, Ungerer, DeVenuto
NOES 0

of the property to share in the roadway maintenance obligations of the existing un-recorded Private
Roadway Easement and Maintenance Agreement. The instrument shall be satisfactory to the zoning
board attorney. No building permit will be issued until this condition is satisfied.

name: Woodview Heights/39M LLC ~
raw_block:
Discuss decision for area variances of proposed 13 lot subdivision from the definition of “lot area” in
Section 310-2(1)C that requires certain steep slopes and wetland deductions in computing “lot area” and that 80%
of the slopes on each lot have slopes of 15% or less. Said property is located in the R1A Zoning District at the
corner of Hill Avenue and Van Buren Court in Highland Mills and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax maps as
Section 204 Block 1 Lot 34. ,
The following responses were provided in the “Statement of Ownership and Interest” provided by the
applicant for a use variance and the Boards decision on each question follows:
1. Will the granting of this variance produce an undesirable change in the character of the neighborhood or
create a detriment to nearby properties? The applicant stated the subject parcel will be developed as a
residential subdivision which is permitted in the R-1A Zoning District. The slopes on the property will not
create any unusual or undesirable feature or condition that would be a detriment to nearby properties.
The Board was ail in agreement that it will not cause a detrimental impact.
2. Can the benefit you seek be achieved by some other feasible method other than variance? The applicant
stated there is no other method by which the applicant can obtain a lot count that will justify the expense
of developing the property as a residential subdivision without the granting of the requested variance.
The Board stated they believe other methods are feasible, and that the applicant has not tried any
other option. They disagree with the applicant.
3. How substantial is the variance that you are requesting? The applicant stated the requested variance is
not substantial, especially when considering the totality of the circumstances. First, the lot count on the
approved plat met the slope regulations in effect when approved. Although not a strict as the current
regulations, the prior slope regulations were complied with and were d

Continuation of Public Hearing to amend conditions on previously approved Zoning Board area
variance dated July 2015 for a new single family dwelling. The condition as noted in the approved
decision being “the home shall not exceed 40’ by 30’. Additionally it shall not have more than two
stories and will contain no basement. it shall include an attached 2-car garage. There shall be a
maximum of 3 bedrooms.” The applicant proposes to construct a 4 bedroom single family dwelling that
is 34 ft. by 40 ft. with a basement and eliminate the 2 car garage. No further variances would be
required. Said property is located in the R2A Zoning District at 3 Second Street (vacant lot at the corner
of Schunnemunk Rd) in Highland Mills and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax maps as Section 214
Block 8 Lot 3.
Mr. Neil Frishberg the attorney for this applicant stated there are two issues for the board to
decide. One being whether the board should re-hear the matter as a revision of a pre-existing variance
in which even a unanimous affirmative vote is required, or as a new application for variances due to new
facts or a change in circumstances in which case a majority affirmative vote is required of the board
after hearing the new facts and/or the change in circumstances should change the conditions it had
imposed on the variance it had granted the applicant on July 22, 2015.
Mr. Michael Sandor stated there will be an HVAC system in the basement and the basement
height will not exceed 6 feet. They will basically be using it for mechanical storage. Mr. Malik thanked
the board for the approval three years ago and stated the house has been on the market and he has had
a problem selling it due to people wanting a bigger house for a-larger family with 5-6 children.

it was not needed. She also questioned the cil tank in the basement. G, Kestenbaum stated that most
bi-levels are at grade or below gra

label: A
name: Bakertown Road Condominium
raw_block:
Review Decision for an interpretation pursuant to Section 310-44(c) 3, 4. Applicant proposes to
construct an emergency access road on vacant land, whereas, pursuant to section 310-7 of the bulk
regulations, an emergency access road is not listed as a permitted use. Said property is located in the
R2A Zoning District at the corner of County Route 105 and Acres Road in HM SBL 225-1-1.
This applicant first appeared before this board on May 9, 2018 seeking an interpretation on
whether it may construct an emergency access road on land in the Village of Woodbury to serve a multi-
family residential project in the Village of Kiryas Joel. That evening, Terry Rice, Esq., the applicant’s
attorney, requested an adjournment to submit an opinion letter supporting the application. The public
hearing was then continued to the June meeting. Further adjournments have been granted, each time at
the request of the applicant. The opinion letter has not been provided. On August 8, 2018, Mr. Rice
emailed the zoning board, reporting that the applicant’s “engineer is out of town” and, based upon that,
“respectfully request[ing] a last adjournment to the next available meeting.” Given the extensive delays
and lack of responsiveness of the applicant, this board declines to grant that extension. This board
upholds the refusal of the Village Building Inspector to approve use of the access road.

three-family home.
The applicants’ proposal is set forth on a site plan entitled “Site Plan for Falkowitz” prepared by James A.
Dillin PLS and dated October 25, 2018 [last revision: May 28, 2019]. That plan is hereby incorporated into this
decision and shall remain in the zoning board’s file in this matter.
No members of the public were heard during the hearing.
The Building Inspector issued a determination dated February 27, 2019.
The applicant has appealed the Building Inspector’s determination seeking a special Permit to allow the
demolition of the existing building and reconstruction of a new building with square footage not in excess of 10%
of the existing structure.

label: A
name: Schwimmer
raw_block:
Review decision for an area variance to permit the installation of an 8 feet high solid pool curtain around
an existing in ground swimming pool. Said property is located at 21 Vanderbilt Dr., HM SBL 245- 1 -102.
In reviewing the facts presented for the requested area variances, the Board considered the five
standards for determining whether the applicant has sustained its burden of proof as required by Village Law
Section 7-712-b (c). Each factor has been considered relevant to the decision of the board of appeals, but no
single one is viewed as precluding the granting of the variances.
Based upon the testimony and comments from members of the public the Board determines that an
undesirable change in the character of this neighborhood or detriment to the neighbors in that neighborhood
will result. II is noted that this determination is not based upon the Board's consideration of the Home
Owner's private covenants that require architectural approval from the HOA Board of Directors but rather a
consideration of the neighbors objections to the type of material being used for the fence.
The Board finds that the proposed fence will result in a serious, undesirable detriment to surrounding
property owners.
The Board finds that there are alternatives available to the applicant to meet the perceived need for the
variance because a fence made of complying material would obviate the need for a cloth or canvas material
fence. Also, the applicant failed to demonstrate that a fence 8 feet in height was required to achieve the
required privacy and the Board determined that alternative means were available to achieve that privacy
including umbrellas and screens neither of which would require a variance.
Based upon the foregoing the Board finds that there are other alternatives which would meet the
needs of the applicant making a variance unnecessary.
Considering that the applicant is requesting that the "fence' be permitted as a temporary structure
which is also not permitted, tha

Public Hearing for an amendment to an existing variance granted on September 12, 2018, to permit a tan
insert to a 6ft high solid stockade fence, whereas said variance was granted to permit only white vinyl fencing. A
variance is required pursuant to Section 146-7d, whereas proposed fence will be multi-colored. Said property is
located in the R2A Zoning District at 14 Pheasant Run in Highland Mills and is known on the Village of Woodbury
Tax maps as Section 247 Block 2 Lot 5.
Chairwoman Ungerer, stated this application was in front of the board in 2018, in the decision it states the
applicant was allowed a white fence. The Board voted to rehear the application due to the applicant going to the
building department and requesting a tan fence opposed to the white fence he was allowed in his application. The
Building Department then issued him a building permit for the tan inserts. A resident then complained that Mr.
Sofer put up a tan fence instead of the white that was allowed in his variance. Mr. Sofer was then issued a
violation notice for the tan inserts. Chairwoman Ungerer, then stated the Board received the following letter from
a resident:
“With regard to the matter of the appeal for the variance pursuant to section 146-7d for
David Sofer at 14 Pheasant Run we have numerous concerns. It may seem as some are not
relevant to this case, but we are trying to make a point.
When this permit was first applied for as a chain link fence, they said it was to keep our
dog off their property; it was denied, and they reapplied for a second fence. This fence is not
enclosed and the dog or any animal, domestic or wild could walk around it.
A second application was submitted for a "solid white vinyl fence," the same as ours. A

Pursuant to the foregoing and employing the criteria for an area variance and In employing the balancing
tests set forth in Village Law Section 7-712-b (c), the Board hereby grants the Village Law Section 7-736 variance
and Code Section 310-12 (A)(2) variance as requested upon the following conditions:
1. A recorded Private Roadway Easement and Maintenance Agreement shall be signed by the parties using
the proposed accessway/driveway. This variance approval requires that the applicant join in the mutual
obligations of that agreement, preferably by recording an instrument setting forth the same obligations (or
different obligations satisfactory to all existing parties to the agreement). The recorded instrument shall be
satisfactory to the zoning board attorney. Should the applicant fail to obtain an agreement from those
others to sign a recordable instrument (in such case, written specification of the attempts made to obtain
such agreement must be submitted), then he must record a declaration running with the land announcing
an obligation on the part of the current owner, and all future owners, of the property to share in the
roadway maintenance obligations of the Private Roadway Easement and Maintenance Agreement. The
instrument shall be satisfactory to the zoning board attorney. The applicant shall copy the Zoning Board on
its letter transmitting the Easement and Agreement to the Orange County Clerk for recording and shall
provide the Zoning Board with any return correspondence reflecting either acceptance or rejection of said
deed. No building permit will be issued until this condition is satisfied.
2. No building permit will be issued until proof of the Easement and Maintenance Agreement recording are

label: B
name: Hasan
raw_block:
Review draft decision for an area variance to permit the construction of an addition to an existing single-
family dwelling leaving a side yard of 20 ft. Whereas, pursuant to Section 310-6(B) and 310-7 District Regulations a
minimum side yard of 30 ft. is required. Property is located in the R1A Zoning District and located at 166 Ridge
Road, HM SBL236-6-4.
Decision:
In employing the balancing tests set forth in Village Law Section 7-712-b (c), the Board hereby grants the
variance as requested upon the following condition(s):
The variance hereby granted is for the purpose of authorizing construction of the addition as shown on
the sketch presented to the Board only. No construction other than for such addition is authorized by this decision
and no future or different construction can occur unless a new variance being granted.
The Board is concerned that the sketch presented may not be too scale and therefore the applicant is to present to
the building department a scaled sketch accurately depicting the bulk area requirements of the zoning district and
the accurate location of the proposed new addition to be no closer to the side yard boundary than 20 feet. It shall
be for the building inspector’s determination as to the sufficiency and accuracy of the required sketch such that
the compliance of the addition with the variance hereby granted can be correctly ascertained.
By roll call a motion to adopt and grant the decision was voted as follows:
AYES 5 Ungerer, Abrams, Brady, DeVenuto, Kestenbaum

The following responses were provided in the “Statement of Ownership and Interest” provided by the
applicant for a use variance and the Boards decision on each question follows:
1. Will the granting of this variance produce an undesirable change in the character of the neighborhood or
create a detriment to nearby properties? The applicant stated: No, the proposed site for the swimming
pool is completely fenced by 6ft vinyl panels and the pool will not be connected in any way to neighboring
properties. The Board Agreed.
2. Can the benefit you seek be achieved by some other feasible method other than variance? The applicant
stated: No. The size and shape of the backyard where the proposed pool is to be located, limit the
location of the pool and does not allow for the installation without imposing on the 20 foot regular
allowance by the Zoning Board. The Board Agreed.
3. How substantial is the variance that you are requesting? The applicant stated: Not substantial – 10 feet
from the property line. The Board Agreed.
4. Will the granting of the variance have an adverse effect or impact on the physical or environmental
conditions in the neighborhood or district? The applicant stated: No, it will have no effect or impact on
the physical or environmental conditions in the neighborhood or district. Since it will be within the fenced
backyard. The Board Agreed.
5. Is the alleged difficulty self-created? The applicant stated: No, the size of the property is relatively small
and the house is so located that the size of the backyard is proportionally small. Also, the shape of the
backyard is a further constraint. The Board Disagree

Public Hearing for a use variance and special permit to demolish and rebuild a dwelling containing a non-
conforming 3 family and replacing it with a 2 family dwelling. Pursuant to Section 310-43.2(B)(3) a non-conforming
use “shall not be changed to another nonconforming use without the approval of the Zoning Board of Appeals and
then only to a use which in the opinion of said Board is of the same or more restrictive in nature.” Furthermore, the
applicant proposes to expand the non-conforming dwelling by up to 10%, whereas, pursuant to Section 310-
43.2(C), the Zoning Board of Appeals may issue a Special Permit for the expansion of a non-conforming use of a
building provided the expansion meets certain criteria set forth in Section 310-43.2(C)(1)(2) of the Village Code.
Said property is located in the R2A Zoning District at 12 Skytop Road in Highland Mills and is known on the Village
of Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 217 Block 3 Lots 4

label: G
name: Kestenbaum withdrew his motion
raw_block:
Mr. Myrow stated that in the beginning of the meeting the Chairwoman Ungerer stated that he would
have the option to wait for a full board.
Motion was then reinitiated by G. Kestenbaum, seconded by Chairwoman Ungerer to carry over and
continue the public hearing to the June 12, 2019 meeting. Chairman Ungerer conducted a roll call of the Board
which resulted in the motion being:
ADOPTED AYES 2 Ungerer, Kestenbaum
NAYS 2 Abrams, DeVenuto
ABSENT 1 Brady
Chairwoman Ungerer stated a tie vote is a no. Attorney Dickover, then stated there is no action on the
motion. The option is to grant the relief, or don’t grant the relief or renew the motion.
Motion was offered by Chairwoman Ungerer, seconded by J. DeVenuto, to close the public hearing.
Chairman Ungerer conducted a roll call of the Board which resulted in the motion being:
ADOPTED AYES 4 Ungerer, Kestenbaum, Abrams, DeVenuto
NAYS 0
ABSENT 1 Brady
Mr. Myrow stated he wants to advise the Board that he will be withdrawing his application in its
entirety.
Mr. Myrow, stated that the board had a lot of questions and he has never not been able to ask for an
adjournment and be granted said adjournment. G. Kestenbaum, stated they have 60 days to make a decision and
allow the applicant to submit any additional information.
Attorney Dickover stated that it would be more economical of the board to keep the public hearing open,
receive whatever documentation that the applicant has to submit and hear what he has to say because if its closed
and Mr. Myrow withdraws he would have to re-submit an application, get another determination from the Building
Inspector and the Board will be right back here a couple of months from now. It may be more expeditious to leave
the matter open and receive the additional materials and then consider the application in its totality at that point.

label: E
name: Pulver/Rushmore Estate Winery
raw_block:
Public Hearing for an area variance to permit the proposed use of a farm winery. Whereas, pursuant to
Section 310-39.1(B), winery access must be to NYS Route 32 and applicant proposes access through Quaker Road
(Village Road) and Ice House Road (Private Road). Said property is located in the R1A Zoning District at 14 Castleton
Drive in Highland Mills and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 202 Block 1 Lot 70 & 79.
The applicants traffic consultant stated an evaluation of the proposed improvements to Ice house Road,
which will improve it from a narrow private road, to a two-lane gravel road designed to meet current standards will
function as an appropriate roadway to accommodate the anticipated traffic to be generated by the proposed
Page 4 – Village of Woodury- Zonin g Board of Appeals - May 8, 2019
Winery on a busy day. The proposed improvements will accommodate this level of traffic with a significant reserve
capacity to accommodate additional traffic. It will accommodate emergency vehicles, patrons, and delivery to the
Winery, as well as the individual homes located on this road. It is his opinion the improved road will be
substantially better than the existing road serving the six homes and the subject property. It was previously noted
that the Ice House Road approach to Quaker Road should be controlled with a Stop sign and maintain appropriate
Intersection sight distance should be provided. It is also important to note that Ice house Road will be improved to
provide a wider pavement and the alignment of the road will be modified to meet current standards. Therefore, it
is important to keep in mind that the existing road alignment and width will be modified to address current
standards. He then stated that other uses that are permitted on this property would generate a lot more traffic, for

Public Hearing to appeal violation order #2019-012 issued by the Building Inspector for exceeding the
permitted height and location limitations for fencing placed in the side and front yard. In the event the ZBA upholds
said violation, the applicant seeks an area variance to permit a portion of the solid 48 inch high fence in the front
yard to remain. Pursuant to Section 146-7(A) of the Village code, fencing in the front yard shall be uniformly less
than 50% solid. Furthermore, the applicant has erected 6 ft. high solid fencing in a side yard, whereas pursuant to
Section 146-5(B) and 146-7(A) fencing in a side yard shall not exceed 4 feet high and shall be less than 50% solid.
Said property is located in the R2A Zoning District at 273 Seven Springs Road in Highland Mills and is known on the
Village of Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 213 Block 1 Lot 67.

decided that the notice of violation did not need to be discussed at this time. The area variance to permit a 6foot
high fence in the side yard was discussed using the following 5 questions:
The following responses were provided in the “Statement of Ownership and Interest” provided by the
applicant for a use variance and the Boards decision on each question follows:

neighborhood or create a detriment to nearby properties? The project will not result in an undesirable

label: B
name: Buckley
raw_block:
Public Hearing for a Special Permit for the use of an existing second dwelling unit for a family member
(Maria Villanyi) pursuant to Section 310-35 of the Village Code. Said property is located in the R1A Zoning District
at 4 Van Buren Court in Highland Mills and is known on the Tax Maps as Section 238 Block 4 Lot 4.
The board discussed that this house was converted to a mother-daughter in 2012, however the applicant
never received a permit for a mother- daughter and it slipped through the cracks until now. A. Zoumas, asked the
applicant if this house was converted to a mother-daughter for the same person that it is being requested for now.
The applicant stated it is. He then went on to say that his mother in law is currently staying with wife’s sister in
Cornwall because his mother in law had a stroke and his wife’s sister doesn’t work and can watch over her all the
time. He then stated that she is getting better and when she does, they will have her come home and live with
them again. |. DeVenuto, expressed concern that Mr. Buckley’s mother in law is not a permanent resident, and
giving a special permit to someone who doesn’t live there full time doesn’t make sense to him. The board
collectively disagreed.
The board then labeled this application a type 2 action under SEQRA, with no further environmental
action required. Chairwoman Ungerer mace a motion, seconded by J. DeVenuto, to close the public hearing.
Chairman Ungerer, conducted a roll call of the Board which resulted in the motion being:
ADOPTED BY ROLL CALL AS FOLLOWS:
Chairman Ungerer: Yes
Board Member Abrams: Yes
Board Member Brady: Yes
Board Member DeVenuto: Yes
Board Member Zoumas: Yes

label: A
name: Garay
raw_block:
Public Hearing for an area variance requested due to a denial of a building permit by the Building Inspector to
permit the use of an above ground pool (12x24) placed in a side yard. Pursuant to Section 30-25(A) a swimming
pool shall only be permitted within a rear yard. Said property is located in the R1A Zoning District at 270 Skyline
Drive in Highland Mills and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 208 Block 2 Lot 15.12.
Mr. Garay stated that the pool is located on the side yard of the house. The water main runs through the back
of his yard and the small space that is left would not leave him enough room for the pool. A. Zoumas asked the
distance of the water main to the back of the house. Mr. Garay stated 12 feet. He then went on to say that it is a
seasonal pool and could be taken down every year, and that he put it up for his children; so, they could have a
place to play. C. Brady stated that it would be a good idea if he did remove it every year otherwise it will just fall
apart. The board members agreed. Chairwoman Ungerer asked what the pool dimensions were, and Mr. Garay
stated 12x24. Attorney Dickover, stated an anonymous letter was received from someone in the neighborhood
that was concerned about the noise level. The board members unanimously felt that kids are kids and there will
always be noise.
Attorney Dickover, stated that the 239 was sent to the county and their determination is that this is a matter
for local determination, the board should also declare itself lead agency and classify this action as a type 2 action
with no further environmental review required.
Attorney Dickover, stated due to this being a public hearing anyone can submit written comments through the
chat feature, then went on to explain how to do it, he also stated the board is live streaming to Facebook and
there is the ability to ask questions there as well.
Chairwoman Ungerer, voiced concerns about the ladder and the safety of it being in the pool all the tim

label: B
name: Luding
raw_block:
Public Hearing for an area variance to permit the installation of a 5 ft. high chain link fence in a side yard.
Where-as pursuant to Section 146-5(B) a maximum height of 4 ft. is permitted. Said property is located in the R2A
Zoning District at 43 DeSanctis Drive in Highland Mills and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax Maps as
Section 218 Block 2 Lot 30.3.
Chairwoman Ungerer, stated she was looking into this application and she sees that there is a different
owner’s name listed of the house. Mrs. Luding stated that her parents own the house and a letter was submitted
to the building department with their consent to put up the fence.
The applicant stated they are looking for a five-foot fence instead of the four-foot fence that is allowed
because their dogs will jump over a four-foot fence. C.Brady, stated she went out to the property and they have a
tree boarder as well as the property being set back from the road. Chairwoman Ungerer, then asked if there will
be any gates and the applicants stated two gates. J. DeVenuto, asked the applicant if they were going to add any
fabric or close off the view or if it was going to be a plain chain link fence. The applicant stated that there will be no
fabric. A. Zoumas clarified that it will just be 36x31 square feet on the side of the house, and the applicant stated
yes.
Attorney Dickover, stated due to this being a public hearing anyone can submit written comments through the
chat feature, then went on to explain how to do it, he also stated the board is live streaming to Facebook and
there is the ability to ask questions there as well. He then went on to say that he sees no written comment on
either platform.

Public Hearing for an area variance to permit a proposed Bed and Breakfast. Where-as said use requires a lot
area of 3 acres and a rear yard setback of 50 feet pursuant to Section 310-6(B) and 310-7 Bulk Table. Said property

is located in the CR Zoning District at 191 Route 32 in Central Valley and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax
Maps as Section 228 Block 10 Lot 7

application. Chairwoman Ungerer, stated that Josh and Christy have a Campbell Hall address and if it is a bed and
breakfast it has to owner occupied. She then went on to say that it is an eight-bedroom house and five rooms will
be reserved for guests. Mr. Kimple then stated the proposal is that they all will be living there; the five rooms will
be reserved for guests and the three rooms for the owners of the property.
Chairwoman Ungerer, stated that a bed and breakfast is a permitted use, however it needs an R3A zoning
requirement. In the rear yard it is presently an non-conforming pre-existing lot but it’s the same requirement as
in the R3A. Mr. Kimple pointed out to the board members, when looking at the plans there will be no substantial

from the neighboring properties, because there is a small sliver of parking that exists on a neighbor’s property and

Public Hearing for an area variance, per denial of building permit, to permit the construction of an 18’ x
36’ in ground pool (accessory structure) in a side yard. Whereas, pursuant to Section 310-11(c), an accessory
structure is only permitted in the rear yard. Said property is located in the R1A Zoning District at 8 Clinton Alley in
Central Valley with a mailing address of 8 Clinton Alley Monroe, NY 10950 and is known on the Village of
Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 254 Block 2 Lot 67.2
Chairwoman Ungerer, stated the applicant’s reasons for this request which include the following; the
backyard retains water from the slope that exists right behind the home which has also caused damage to the
property on prior occasions when the slope caused a collapse of the current smaller retaining wall that was put in
by the developer. The cost of attempting to grade and place retaining walls at least 1.5 to 2 stories high for the
entire length of the pool to hold back the current topography. They would need a trench to provide drainage due
to all the water that comes down the slope, and the overall cost, removal and demolition of the current slope as
well as part of the slope not being on his property would be a problem. If the pool were to be built in the backyard
it would require a variance for a 20-foot lot line separation. Chairwoman Ungerer then stated that the board
received two letters from neighbors stating that the Martinez’s are wonderful neighbors and neither one has
issues with the installation of the pool and that they have their full support.
Chairwoman Ungerer asked if there was a mini mesh fence. Applicant stated yes it will be a four-foot
metal bar fence, with no added fabric or material, he also stated that it is required with an inground pool and is
also allowed by their HOA. K. Abrams stated he went up and looked at the property and has no problem granting
the variance. A. Zoumas stated it was big and flat and that the side yard would be a good location for t

Village Code Section 310-7.1(d)(5) states a hotel shall have frontage on a state or county highway or right of way,
the Building Inspector has determined that the primary frontage for the proposed hotel is on a Village Road
(Turner Rd.). If required, an area variance is requested to permit the construction of a 130 room 4 story hotel on a
Village Road. Said property is located in the HO (Hotel Overlay) and LC (Limited Commercial) Zoning District at 140
Route 32 and 94 Turner Road, CV SBL 226 –1- 5 & 6.2.
The Board is mindful of the request contained in the referral letter from Mr. Lindsay wherein he
requests that the Zoning Board consider the application "for any other relief that may be determined from a
review of the plan submitted." On this request, the Zoning Board demurs in that the Board does not have
jurisdiction on its own behest to identify the need for potential variances in as same must come from "a review of
any decision, order or ruling made by the Code Enforcement Officer" and further, because this Decision ends
with the interpretation made herein, that the Board has not undertaken a comprehensive review of the
project plan which might identify the need for other variances - that being the task of the Village Planning
Board.
The Board makes this Decision on interpretation based upon several facts as presented by the
applicant. They are:
1. Upon division of parcel 5, that the divided parcel will be merged into parcel 6.2 and thereupon will
comprise but one parcel having one tax lot number and there shall be no future subdivision of the
combined parcel once so merged unless the proposed use of the merged parcel is no longer that of a
hotel.
2. That upon merger of the divided parcel 5 with parcel 6, that said combined parcel will be owned by only one
entity and ownership shall remain thereafter by one singular entity and shall not thereafter be divide

parents would be with a bedroom, a tv/living room. He also stated that their house in skyline is 2,600 square feet

member (Ann Harris), pursuant to Section 310-35 of the Village Code. Said property is located in the R1A Zoning

Chairwoman Ungerer stated that the applicant moved into this house a little over a year ago and is
looking to continue the use of the existing mother-daughter house. She then asked Mr. Balsome if its both floors
or just the upper portion of the house, he stated it is a side by side living arrangement with the bottom level being

asked if this was going to be his mother-in-law’s permanent residence and Mr. Balsome stated yes.
Chairwoman Ungerer stated that the board received a letter from a Ms. Eleanor stating she is in favor of

family, property and are very good neighbors.

pursuant to Section 310-35 of the Village Code. Said property is located in the R1A Zoning District at 20 Abrams
Road in Central Valley and is known on the Tax Maps as Section 226 Block 1 Lot 37.221.
Chairwoman Ungerer stated the applicant states that this house is an existing mother daughter and Ms.
Valdes enclosed a diagram, but she cannot figure out what was the existing house and what is in the second
dwelling and where it is located within the house. Ms. Valdes stated it is in the lower portion of the house. Ms.
Valdes then went on to say that it was a family emergency when her daughter lost her job and then she lost her
own mother to COVID, her daughter moved in so she could help take care of her five-year-old granddaughter. Ms.
Valdes stated she bought the house in 2016 and never used it as a mother-daughter. J. DeVenuto, then asked Ms.
Valdes if the apartment takes up the entire bottom floor of the house. Ms. Valdes stated she uses part of the
downstairs for her own personal office space. The board asked if there was ever a special permit issued or was this
a mother-daughter house prior to the incorporation of the Village ZBA. They then asked the applicant to submit a
drawing to scale and the total square footage of the apartment.
Chairwoman Ungerer then asked if anyone from the public had anything to say. No comments were
received from the Facebook, zoom or public comment features.

the expansion of a non-conforming seasonal dwelling. Proposed expansion of 87 sq. ft. will exceed the maximum

Code. Said property is located in the R2A Zoning District at 31 Seven Springs Road (Carmel Park) in Highland Mills

Chairwoman Ungerer asked what the small addition was on the side of the house, and Mr. Stein stated it
was the boiler. She then stated it looks like a porch was added at one point. Mr. Stein said yes. Chairwoman
Ungerer then stated it looks like you want to square off the entire thing which would add 87 square feet to the
house. Chairwoman Ungerer then stated it is basically a 16% expansion rather than a 10% expansion. Mr. Stein
then stated it is a little more than then the 10% that is allowed with a special permit. He was not counting the
entire house – just the expansion. He then stated he feels that this would make the area look nicer due to them
looking like cabins now. Chairwoman Ungerer asked the board if they had any comments.

Chairwoman Ungerer stated that last month Mr. Stein stated that there were other bungalows in the area
that had gotten variances to expand, however she did check on that with the building department and she didn’t
find any that were given variances. The only one was a request that was made in 2008, but later the applicant
withdrew their application. The board then discussed with the applicant that the Village code states that the
maximum variance that can be given out by the Zoning Board of Appeals is 10%. The board then asked the
applicant to amend their application and submit a new diagram to the building. The applicant then asked if he is
asking for the 10% variance would he still have to come in front of the board. Chairwoman Ungerer stated yes he
does.

representative and there are no comments on the Facebook chat.
Motion was offered by K. Abrams, seconded by J. DeVenuto, to continue the application but have the
applicant submit an amended application to the building department at least a week prior to the next meeting.

label: E
name: Marin
raw_block:
Public Hearing for a use variance to permit the existing accessory structure (16 ft. x 16 ft. storage shed) to
remain on a vacant unoccupied lot. Whereas, pursuant to Section 310-11(A) “no accessory building or structure
shall be erected prior to any principal building.” Said property is located in the IB Zoning District at 28 Falkirk
Avenue in Central Valley and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 226 Block 1 Lot 63.
Chairwoman Ungerer asked if the applicant was present and if he was the owner of the vacant lot. Mr.
Marin responded yes. Chairwoman Ungerer then stated that the applicant was not allowed to have a shed there
without a house. She then went on to explain that a use variance is very different from the variances the board
had been discussing throughout the rest of the meeting. She then went over all the criteria of a use variance and
what he needed to supply to the board prior to going forward with the application. She explained that the
applicant must show the board that by not allowing a shed there that it would create an unnecessary hardship and
at the same time show every other use that is permitted in the zoning district for that property using real dollars
and cents. She explained that use variances are usually very hard to get, and she wanted the applicant to be aware
of everything he needed. Attorney Naughton then stated use variances are very hard to obtain and nothing like
the special permits or the area variances. She stated unlike area variances that have five different factors that are
state mandated, there is no balancing when trying to obtain a use variance. She then explained the use variance to
the applicant and stated it would be up to him if he was going to proceed. The applicant then stated he was not
going to proceed. Attorney Naughton stated that the board should keep the public hearing open until the June
meeting and that she will write a letter to the applicant so that he is fully aware of what he needs to submi

label: A
name: Yitzchok Stein/Ideal Design
raw_block:
Review decision for an area variance based on a denial of a building permit by the Code Official to permit
the expansion of a non-conforming seasonal dwelling. Said property is located in the R2A Zoning District at 31
Seven Springs Road (Carmel Park) in HM SBL 216-5-1.1.
DECISION:
Village Code § 310-43.2(C) ("Nonconforming Uses"): Expansion of Nonconforming Use - Special Permit
"[T]he nonconforming use of buildings or land lawfully existing on July 11, 1990 .., or the nonconforming
use of buildings or land lawfully existing on the day immediately preceding the effective date of any amendment to
this chapter affecting such use, regardless of change of title possession or occupancy or right thereof, may be
continued indefinitely, except that such use... [s]hall not be enlarged, altered, extended or restored...." Village
Code § 310-43.2(B)(l). However, pursuant to Village Code § 310-43.2(C), the Board ''may issue a special permit for
the expansion of a nonconforming use of a building by up to 10% of the floor area of the principal structure,
provided it finds that: (i) Said expansion of the use is not detrimental to surrounding properties or to the
health, safety and general welfare of the immediate neighborhood; and (ii) No need for an expansion of off-
street parking or traffic generation will occur in a residential district as a result of such an expanded use."
As stated above, the use of property as seasonal dwellings is not permitted in the R-2A district; however,
the Building Inspector has determined that this is a legally pre-existing, nonconforming use.
In connection with the first element, whether such "expansion of the use is not detrimental to surrounding
properties or to the health, safety and general welfare of the immediate neighborhood," the Board found
that the enlargement is very small and adds some living space. The Board determined that the modest
expansion does not alter the character of the community and is not detrimental to surrounding properties
geo_display_name: 31, Seven Springs Road, Lebanon, Village of Woodbury, Town of Woodbury, Orange County, New York, 10930, United States
