DECISION :
§310-7: Minimum Lot Area for 1-Family Dwelling: Area Variance
§310-7: Minimum Side Yard Setback: Area Variance
The Village Code requires a minimum of 43,560 square feet (1 acre) per lot, and a minimum side yard
setback of thirty (30) feet for a single-family dwelling and accessory garages. See Village Code § 310-7 and Chapter
310 Attachment 3.! The Applicant is proposing to subdivide a 2.07-acre parcel into two lots as follows: Lot 1 will
consist of 0.91 acres for construction of a new single-family dwelling, and Lot 2 will consist of 1.16 acres and contain
the existing buildings that are proposed to remain (2.5-story dwelling and a portion of masonry building that will be
- converted to a garage). The proposed subdivision will result in the existing masonry buitding that will be converted
into. a garage being located only 12,2 feet from the proposed -side lot line.
Consistent with its statutory obligations under New York State Village Law § 7-712-b when considering an
area variance, the Board balanced the benefit to the Applicant as weighed against the detriment to the health, safety
and welfare of the neighborhood or community if the requested variances were granted. Further, as also required
by statute, the Board took into consideration the following five issues in its balancing test:
1. Whether an undesirable change would be produced in the character of the neighborhood, or a
detriment to nearby properties would be created, by the granting of the requested area variance.

§ 146-5(B): Height Limitations: Area Variance
§ 146-7{A}: Materials and Composition: Area Variance
Village Code § 146-5(8} provides that “{nlo fence shall be more than four feet in height in any front yard
and side yard.” Village Code § 146-7{A) provides that “no fence shall be erected in a front yard in a residential district
or along a public right-of-way unless the fence is uniformly less than 50% solid.” The Applicant is requesting
variances from these two provisions to permit him to relocate an existing solid six (6) foot tall fence in the front yard
along the public right-of-way of Adrianne Drive. As stated supra, this property is considered-a “corner lot” under
the Village Code and, therefore, has two front yards ~ along Adrianne Drive and Cindy Lane. ,

Review draft decision for variances for the construction of a single-family residence. Whereas pursuant
to Section 310-7, properties in the R-2A district are required to have a minimum lot width of 175 feet, and
pursuant to Section 310-12, properties in the R-2A district are required to have a minimum street frontage of one
hundred (100) feet. The application proposes a minimum lot width of fifty (50) feet and 81.75 feet of street
frontage. Said property is located at 372 Route 32, CV SBL 218-1-42.2.
DECISION :
§ 310-7 and Schedule of District Regulations, R-2A District (Chapter 310 Attachment 2}: Minimum Lot Width:
Area Variance ‘
§ 310-12(A}: Minimum Street Frontage: Area Variance
Village Code § 310-2(B) defines. “lot width” as the “minimum distance between the side lines of a lot,
measured at the required front yard setback line.” The Village Code requires a minimum lot width of 175 feet per
lot containing a permitted use in the R-2A district. See Village Code § 310-7 and Chapter 310 Attachment 2.3 The
Applicants seek to construct a 1-family dwelling on an existing vacant lot having a Jot width of fifty (50) feet, thus
requiring a variance of 125 feet. Additionally, Village Code § 310-12(A}(2) provides that in all districts other than
the R-0.25 district, “not less than 100 feet of street frontage shall be required.” The Applicants’ lot has an existing
street frontage of 81.75 feet, thus requiring a variance of 18.25 feet. :
Consistent with jts statutory obligations under New York State Village Law § 7-712-b when considering an
area variance, the Board balanced the benefit to the Applicants as weighed against the detriment to the health,
safety and welfare of the neighborhood or community if the requested variances were granted. Further, as also
required by statute, the Board took into consideration the following five issues in its balancing test:
1. Whether an undesirable change would be produced in the character of the neighborhood, or a
' detriment to nearby p

Village Code § 310-32(B)(2): Maximum Square Footage of Single Retail Establishment –
Area Variance
Pursuant to Village Code § 310-32(B)(2), retail shops, “as permitted in Item 9 under ‘special permit uses’ in
the Schedule of Zoning District Regulations, LC District, are restricted as follows: retail shops limited to the sale of
food, beverages and miscellaneous small items, including convenience stores; … , provided that . . . [n]o single retail
establishment shall exceed 8,000 square feet, except a supermarket, which shall not exceed 40,000 square feet.”
The Applicant is proposing to construct a 12,860.5 square foot structure containing a single retail establishment for
the sale of beverages (with accessory storage and recycling),1 requiring a variance of 4,860.5 square feet.

statute, the Board took into consideration the following five issues in its balancing test:

Village Code § 146-5(B) provides that “[n]o fence shall be more than four feet in height in any front yard

or along a public right-of-way unless the fence is uniformly less than 50% solid.” The Board is authorized to grant
variances from Chapter 146 under Village Code § 146-9. The Applicant is requesting variances from these two
provisions to permit him to construct a solid six (6) foot tall fence in the front yard along the public right-of-way of
New York State Route 32. As stated supra, this property is considered a “corner lot” under the Village Code and,
therefore, has two front yards – along Ridge Road and along New York State Route 32.

that sandwiches offerings are nothing new and that the Village previously issued a Certificate of Occupancy based
on plans that include equipment for sandwich preparation. He then stated sandwiches are only a small portion of
the sales; coffee and donuts remain its core business. He then stated that their position will always remain that
Dunkin Donuts is a retail business with or without a drive thru.

label: A
name: Southside Oil/Dunkin Donuts
raw_block:
Review decision requesting an interpretation of Sections 310-2 and 310-7 and whether adding a drive-
thru to the existing Dunkin Donuts is permitted. Said property is located in the LC Zoning District at 124 Route 32
in CV SBL 226 1-80.
DECISION
in connection with the properties located at 124 Route 32, Central Valley, New York, the Applicant
requested an interpretation from the Building Inspector regarding whether the Dunkin Donuts was a “fast-food
restaurant” or a “retail business” under the Zoning Code. The Building Inspector issued an email dated April-25,
2022, stating:
My interpretation on this is that on its own, not by creating a drive through, Dunkin Donuts is
considered “Fast-Food” as defined within the Village Code, section 310-2:
RESTAURANT, DRIVE-IN OR FAST-FOOD
Any establishment whose principal business is the sale of foods, frozen desserts, or beverages to
the customer in a ready-to-consume state, usually served in paper, plastic, or other disposable containers
for consumption within the restaurant building, elsewhere on the premises, or for carry-out, for
consumption off the premises.
My reason for this is the over the years as Dunkin Donuts transformed from just a place to get
coffee and a donut or bagel, to a place where one can purchase a variety of hot sandwiches and wraps
prepared on site. The products they sell are made all day long.
On May 25, 2022, the Applicant timely appealed the Building Inspector’s determination to this Board. See
Village Code § A316-6(B) (“An appeal must be made within 30 days of the action of the administrative official
appealed from.”}
As provided in New York Village Law, a ZBA’s appellate powers and discretion are as broad as the Building
inspector's initial scope of review, and it is free to make whatever decision it believes “ought to have been made”
by the Building Inspector. See Village Law § 7-712-b(1). The Board was aware of how it is not the role of the Board
to ‘negotiate’ the use of the property with the Appl

DECISION
Village Code § 310-11(C), Permitting Accessory Building in Rear Yards Only: Area Variance
Village Code § 310-12(C), Required yards and open spaces for detached accessory buildings: Area Variance
Village Code § 310-11(C) provides that “[a]n accessory building or use may be permitted only in a rear yard.”
Village Code § 310-12(C)(3) provides that “Any required yard shall be open space and entirely free of buildings other
than . . . A detached accessory building occupying not more than 25% of a required yard and set back from any lot
line ½ the height of such accessory building, but in no case less than six feet.” The Applicants are seeking two area
variances to construct an accessory storage shed located six (6) feet from the side property line that occupies 40%
of the required setback area.
Consistent with its statutory obligations under New York State Village Law § 7-712-b when considering an
area variance, the Board balanced the benefit to the Applicants as weighed against the detriment to the health,
safety and welfare of the neighborhood or community if the requested variance was granted. Further, as also
required by statute, the Board took into consideration the following five issues in its balancing test:
1. Whether an undesirable change would be produced in the character of the neighborhood, or a
detriment to nearby properties would be created, by the granting of the requested area variances.
2. Whether the benefit sought by the Applicants could be achieved by some method, feasible for the

Whereas pursuant to Section 310-43.2(C), nonconforming uses may be expanded by up to 10% of the floor area of
the principal structure, subject to a special permit and certain findings of the Zoning Board of Appeals. Said
property is located in the R-3A Zoning District along Schunnemunk Road in Highland Mills and is known on the
Village of Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 213, Block 1, Lot 1.11
DECISION
Village Code § 310-43.2(C) (“Nonconforming Uses”): Expansion of Nonconforming Use – Special Permit
Village Code § 310-43.2(B)(1) provides that “the nonconforming use of buildings or land lawfully existing on
July 11, 1990…, or the nonconforming use of buildings or land lawfully existing on the day immediately preceding
the effective date of any amendment to [Chapter 310 of the Village Code] affecting such use, regardless of change
of title possession or occupancy or right thereof, may be continued indefinitely, except that such use… [s]hall not be
enlarged, altered, extended or restored…except as provided in [Village Code § 310-43.2(C).” Pursuant to Village Code
§ 310-43.2(C), the Board “may issue a special permit for the expansion of a nonconforming use of a building by up
to 10% of the floor area of the principal structure, provided it finds that: (1) Said expansion of the use is not
detrimental to surrounding properties or to the health, safety and general welfare of the immediate neighborhood;
and (2) No need for an expansion of off-street parking or traffic generation will occur in a residential district as a
result of such an expanded use.”
As stated above, the current use of Property as seasonal dwellings is not permitted in the R-3A district;
however, the Building Inspector has determined that this is a legally pre-existing, nonconforming use. The Applicant
has applied for a special permit under Village Code § 310-43.2(C) to expand one of the existing nonconfor

Section 310-11, accessory buildings may be permitted only in a rear yard, and whereas pursuant to Section 310-
12(C)(3), any required yard shall be open space and entirely free of buildings other than a detached accessory
building occupying not more than 25% of a required yard and setback from any lot line ½ the height of such
accessory building, but in no case less than six feet. Said property is located in the R-0.25A Zoning District along
Park Avenue in Highland Mills and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 220, Block 3, Lot 24.
DECISION
Village Code § 310-11(C), Permitting Accessory Building in Rear Yards Only: Area Variance
Village Code § 310-11(C) provides that “[a]n accessory building or use may be permitted only in a rear yard.”
The Applicants are seeking an area variance to construct an accessory storage shed located within the front yard, as
determined by the Building Inspector. The proposed shed is 10’ x 16’ (160 square feet) and has a height of 10.8 feet.
The shed is proposed to be set back 50-feet from the front lot line and seven (7) feet from the side lot line. Thus, the
shed does not require any additional variance(s) from Village Code § 310-12(C)(3).1
Consistent with its statutory obligations under New York State Village Law § 7-712-b when considering an
area variance, the Board balanced the benefit to the Applicant as weighed against the detriment to the health, safety
and welfare of the neighborhood or community if the requested variance was granted. Further, as also required by
statute, the Board took into consideration the following five issues in its balancing test:
1. Whether an undesirable change would be produced in the character of the neighborhood, or a
detriment to nearby properties would be created, by the granting of the requested area variance.

property is in the R-2A Zoning District on the corner of First Avenue and Schunnemunk Road in HM and
is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 214, Block 6, Lot 5.
DECISION
Village Code § 310-43, Nonconforming Buildings: Discussion
Under Village Code § 310-43(B), “a building lawfully existing on July 11, 1990 (the day immediately
preceding the effective date of the Town predecessor to this chapter), or lawfully existing on the day
immediately preceding the effective date of any amendment to this chapter affecting such building,
regardless of change of title, possession or occupancy or right thereof, may be continued indefinitely,
subject to [certain identified restrictions].” The single-family dwelling, constructed in the 1950s, is a
nonconforming building as it does not conform to the dimensional requirements of the Zoning Code
(i.e., lot area, setbacks, etc.). The Applicant seeks to demolish the seasonal dwelling and construct a
year-round dwelling in substantially the same location. The demolition of the existing structure removes
all rights the Applicant may have to any legally pre-existing nonconforming bulk and dimensional
conditions. Similarly, the abandonment of the nonconforming seasonal dwelling use removes all rights
the Applicant may have to any legally pre-existing nonconforming use. See Village Code § 310-43.2.
Village Code § 310-43.1, Nonconforming Lots of Record: Discussion
In addition to the nonconforming nature of the seasonal dwelling building, as noted above, the
Applicant’s property is a nonconforming lot, which is defined in the Village Code as a “lot, the area or
dimension of which was lawful prior to the adoption, revision or amendment of a zoning ordinance but
which fails to conform to the requirements of the zoning district in wh

label: B
name: 50 Smith Clove LLC
raw_block:
Review draft decision for the conversion of an existing laundromat to residential apartments.
Said property is located in the HB Zoning District along Smith Clove Road in Central Valley and is known
on the Village of Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 231, Block 3, Lot 3.
DECISION
Village Code § 310-6(F): Combined Minimum Lot Area – Area Variance
Village Code § 310-7, Schedule of Zoning District Regulations, HB District [Chapter 310 Attachment 7])
– Minimum Lot Area: Area Variance
Village Code § 310-40(E): Off-Street Parking – Area Variance
Village Code § 310-42(E)(2): Supplementary Regulations – Area Variance
Page 4 – Zoning Board of Appeals Minutes - April 12, 2023
Village Code § 310-6(F) provides that “[e]xcept as otherwise permitted (commercial center, industrial
park, etc.), where more than one principal use occupies a lot, said lot shall be large enough to satisfy the
minimum lot area or density requirements of each use.” On the HB Schedule of District Regulations, a
single-family dwelling must have a minimum lot area of 10,000 square feet, and each dwelling unit must
have 6,000 square feet. See Village Code § 310-7 and Chapter 310 Attachment 7, Permitted Use No. 1
[referring to Chapter 310 Attachment 4, Permitted Use No. 1] and Special Permit Use No. 15. The
Applicant proposes to maintain the existing single-family dwelling and convert the laundromat into two
(2) dwelling units. This requires a total minimum lot area of 22,000 square feet, and the property only
consists of 11,753 square feet. Therefore, the Applicant is requesting a variance of 10,247 square feet.
Pursuant to Village Code § 310-40(E), “[n]o off-street parking shall be permitted within a required front
yard in any district….” The Applicant proposes to have one parking stall located partially within the
required front yard along Smith Clove Road and is thus requesting a variance for this parking stall.
Additionally, Village Code § 310-42(E)(2) requires driveways for parking areas with ten (10) spaces
geo_display_name: Smith Clove Road, Central Valley, Village of Woodbury, Town of Woodbury, Orange County, New York, 10917, United States

Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 228, Block 6, Lot 7.
DECISION
Village Code § 310-43, Nonconforming Buildings: Discussion
Under Village Code § 310-43(B), “a building lawfully existing on July 11, 1990 (the day immediately
preceding the effective date of the Town predecessor to this chapter), or lawfully existing on the day
immediately preceding the effective date of any amendment to this chapter affecting such building,
regardless of change of title, possession or occupancy or right thereof, may be continued indefinitely,
subject to [certain identified restrictions].” The Applicant’s property previously contained a single-
family dwelling that was constructed in 1912 and constituted a nonconforming building, as it did not
conform to the bulk table or dimensional requirements of the Zoning Code (i.e., lot area, setbacks, etc.).
As noted above, the single-family dwelling was destroyed in a fire, and the Applicant seeks to
reconstruct it.
Reconstruction of a damaged nonconforming building is governed by Village Code § 310-43(B)(2)(a),
which provides as follows: “A nonconforming building (whether housing a conforming or a
nonconforming use) which has been damaged by fire or other causes to the extent of more than 50% of
its fair market value shall not be repaired or rebuilt unless such building is made to conform to the
height and yard requirements of the Bulk Table and other dimensional regulations of this chapter.” The
Building Inspector has determined that the Applicant’s dwelling has been damaged by fire “to the extent
of more than 50% of its fair market value.” See Village Code § 310-43(B)(2)(a). As a result, the dwelling
cannot be repaired or rebuilt unless it is brought into compliance with the Code. The Applicant is thus
requesting variances from certain applicable bulk

DECISION
§ 310-30(D): Signs: Interpretation
Section 310-30(D)(2){c}[1] of the Vilage Code provides that “[t]he area of a sign shail be computed from
the geometric area of the actual sign configuration, be it square, rectangle, circle, oval or other polygon
shape. The area.shall be measured from the outer dimensions of the frame, trim, or molding by which
the sign is enclosed where they exist, or from the outer edge of the signboard where they do not exist.”
Similarly, Section 310-30(D)(2}(c)}{[4)} of the Village Code provides that “[t]he area of supporting
frameworki,f any, such as brackets or posts,-shall not be included.in the area if such frameworki s
incidentali n the display.”
The Applicant proposes to construct an approximately 280 square foot multitenant directory pylon sign,
consisting of approximately 140 square feet of text and the remainder stone veneer. On February 24,
2023, the Building Inspector opined that the stone veneer framework proposed as part of the sign
should be included in the calculation of sign area based upon Village Code § 310-30(D)(2}(c)[1], Le.., that
the stone veneer is the ‘outer dimensions of the frame.’ The Applicant has argued that the stone veneer
is “supporting framework” and, therefore, should not be included in the calculation of the sign area
pursuant to Village Code § 310-30(D}(2}{4). The Applicant further argued that the stone veneer does not
meet the definition of a “sign” in the Code because it does not convey massages, directions, or
advertisements to the public.?

DECISION
Village Code § 310-35 (“Dwelling Unit For Additional Family Member”): Special Permit
Village Code § 310-35 provides as follows:
“A temporary special permit may be granted by the Zoning Board of Appeals for an additional dwelling
unit for the use of family members in accordance with the following:
Additional unit may only be used for up to two additional persons directly related to the owners of the
principal dwelling unit;
The single-family dwelling may be expanded up to 25% to a maximum of 1,000 square feet of the floor
area of the principal dwelling unit to accommodate the new unit;
All bulk dimensional requirements of the zoning district in which the dwelling is located must be
complied with;
In the event the property is either sold or the additional unit is vacated, the permit will become null and
void. The property shall be restored to its original single-family status and permanent cooking facilities
(i.e., stove) shall be removed;
The applicant (property owner) shall provide to the Zoning Board of Appeals names of all persons who
intend to occupy the additional unit. Homeowner and intended occupants shall sign the special permit
upon its approval by the ZBA; and
All facilities are subject to an annual inspection by the Building Department.”

of the porte cochère. The Applicant is proposing two (2) additional wall signs as follows: (i) one “Hampton
Inn” sign on the southeastern facade; and (ii) one “Welcome” sign on the porte cochère.

warning, or informational signs, including but not limited to “exit,” “entrance,” “parking” or “one-way.”
Such signs shall not exceed three square feet each.” On June 8, 2023, the Building Inspector determined

in width. The bottom edge of a freestanding sign shall be at least seven feet above the ground elevation
when located in an area where it would impede pedestrian mobility and/or impair visibility, as
determined by the Code Enforcement Officer. Freestanding signs must be a minimum of 12 feet from
the edge of street pavement. Only one freestanding sign shall be permitted on a lot, except as otherwise
specified by this chapter.”

permit and certain findings of the Zoning Board of Appeals. Said property is located in the R-3A Zoning
District along Schunnemunk Road in Highland Mills and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax Maps
as Section 213, Block 1, Lot 1.11.
DECISION
Village Code § 310-43.2(C) (“Nonconforming Uses”): Expansion of Nonconforming Use – Special Permit
Village Code § 310-43.2(B)(1) provides that “the nonconforming use of buildings or land lawfully
existing on July 11, 1990…, or the nonconforming use of buildings or land lawfully existing on the day
immediately preceding the effective date of any amendment to [Chapter 310 of the Village Code] affecting
such use, regardless of change of title possession or occupancy or right thereof, may be continued
indefinitely, except that such use… [s]hall not be enlarged, altered, extended or restored…except as
provided in [Village Code § 310-43.2(C).” Pursuant to Village Code § 310-43.2(C), the Board “may issue a
special permit for the expansion of a nonconforming use of a building by up to 10% of the floor area of
the principal structure, provided it finds that: (1) Said expansion of the use is not detrimental to
surrounding properties or to the health, safety and general welfare of the immediate neighborhood; and
(2) No need for an expansion of off-street parking or traffic generation will occur in a residential district
as a result of such an expanded use.”
As stated above, the current use of Property as seasonal dwellings is not permitted in the R-3A
district; however, the Building Inspector has determined that this is a legally pre-existing, nonconforming
use. The Applicant has applied for a special permit under Village Code § 310-43.2(C) to expand o

§ 146-5(8): Height Limitations: Area Variance
§ 146-7{A}: Materials and Composition: Area Variance
Village Code § 146-5(B) provides that “[nlo fence shall be more than four feet in height in any
front yard and side yard.” Village Code § 146-7(A) provides that “no fence shail be erected in a front yard
in a residential district or along-a public right-of-way unless the fence is uniformly less than 50% solid.”
The Applicants are requesting variances from these two provisions to permit the construction of a solid
six (6) foot tall fence in the side yard, and the property is located along the public right-of-way of Florance
Drive.
Consistent with its statutory obligations under New York State Village Law § 7-712-b when
considering an area variance, the Board balanced the benefit to the Applicants as weighed against the
detriment-to the health, safety and welfare of the neighborhood or community if the requested variances
were granted. Further, as also required by statute, the Board took into consideration the following five
issues in its balancing test:
1. Whether an undesirable change would be producedi n the character of the neighborhood, or
a detriment to nearby properties would be created, by the granting of the requested area
variances.
2. Whether the benefit sought by the Applicants could be achieved by some method, feasible
for the Applic

Village Cede § 310-43.1, Nonconforming Lots of Record: Discussion
As noted above, the Applicant’s property is a nonconforming lot, which is defined in the Village
Code as a “lot, the area or dimension of which was lawful prior to the adoption, revision ar amendment
of a zoning ordinance but which fails to conform to the requirements of the zoning district in which it is
located by reason of such adoption, revision or amendment.” Village Code § 310-2. The property does
not meet the minimum lot area of two (2) acres, and the single-family dwelling that the Applicant seeks
to construct does not meet certain setback requirements.
Village Code § 310-43.1(A) provides, “[a] single residential lot separated by other land not in the
same ownership and made nonconforming as to area or width by the 2011 revisions to this chapter, and
not otherwise vested as to other zoning by state statutory or common law, may be used for a single-family
dwelling, provided that the lot complies with the setback requirements of the district in which it is
located.” The property is separated by other land not in the same ownership and was made
nonconforming as to area by the 2011 revisions to the Village Zoning Chapter. However, the proposed
dwelling does not comply with the setback requirements of the R-2A district. Thus, the Applicant is not
able to const

Village Code § 310-7, Schedule of Zoning District Regulations, R-1A District [Chapter 310 Attachment 3])

Pursuant to Village Code § 310-7, the Schedules of Zoning District Regulations are a part of the
Zoning Chapter and set forth the minimum or maximum zoning bulk and use requirements. The
Schedule of Zoning District Regulations for the R-1A District (Chapter 310, Attachment 3) permits a
maximum lot coverage 20%.1 The Applicant is proposing to construct a 751 square foot basement and
1 The property involved was approved as part of a cluster subdivision in 1986, at which time the
property was located in the R-2 District. The subdivision plat that was filed with the Orange
County Clerk’s Office did not specify coverage requirements. On July 11, 2023, the Building
Inspector issued a determination that the maximum permitted lot coverage would be 20%,

first-floor addition onto his existing single-family dwelling, which will result in a lot of coverage of 43%.
Therefore, the Applicant is requesting a variance of 23%.2

considering an area variance, the Board balanced the benefit to the Applicant as weighed against the

name: 26 Stainton Fareway

Review Decision requesting a variance for the construction of a roof over the front porch on an
single-family dwelling. Whereas pursuant to Section 310-36(D)(2) and 310-7, the minimum front yard
setback is twenty-five (25) feet, and the Applicant is proposing 19.9 feet. Said property is located in the
SH Zoning District at 26 Stainton Fareway in Central Valley and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax
Maps as Section 255, Block 1, Lot 81.
DECISION
Village Code § 310-7, Schedule of Zoning District Regulations, R-0.252A District [Chapter 310 Attachment
4]) – Minimum Front Yard Setback: Area Variance
The Applicants’ property was approved as part of a senior housing section of a cluster subdivision
in 2007 and is part of a senior citizen for-sale, individually owned housing project. Village Code § 310-
36(D)(2) provides that “[i]n a senior citizen for-sale, individually owned housing project, the lot size and
bulk provisions of the R-0.25 Zoning District shall apply….” The bulk provisions of the R-0.25 district require
a minimum front yard setback of twenty-five (25) feet for single family dwellings. See Village Code § 310-
7 and Chapter 310 Attachment 4 - Schedule of Zoning District Regulations for the R-0.25A District.
The Applicants propose to construct a roof over the existing front porch on their dwelling, which
is located 19.9 feet from the front lot line. The existing porch was not subject to the 25-foot front yard
setback pursuant to Village Code § 310-12(C)(1), which allows “[a]n unenclosed entrance porch and steps
not to exceed 70 square feet [in any required yard] provided that it is no closer than 10 feet to the front
lot line.” As the Applicants are proposing to enclose the front porch with a roof, the exception in Village
Code § 310-12(C)(1) will no longer apply and a front yard setback of 25-feet is required. The Applicants
are thus requesting a variance of 5.1 feet to allow a front yard setback of 19.9 feet.

Review Draft Decision requesting an area variance for the construction of two (2) wall signs from

DECISION
§§ 310-30(D)(1)(d), 310-30(K) and Chapter 310 Attachment 13 (310 Attachment 11): Signage Table: Area
Variances
Village Code § 310-30(D)(2)(d) provides that “[p]roperties shall not be permitted more than two
of the following types of signage, as listed on the signage table: wall, projecting, freestanding, multitenant,
canopy, and outside marquee.” Additionally, the Signage Table, Attachment 13 to Chapter 310 (a/k/a 310
Attachment 11) provides that wall signs in the IB District are permitted to be a maximum of “10% of total
wall area/1 per use”.
The Applicant is requesting permission to construct two (2) wall signs, which would result in one
(1) wall sign greater than what is allowed by the Code. Each sign is proposed to be 45.81 square feet, and
thus appears to comply with the 10% limitation.
Consistent with its statutory obligations under New York State Village Law § 7-712-b when
considering an area variance, the Board balanced the benefit to the Applicant as weighed against the
detriment to the health, safety and welfare of the neighborhood or community if the requested variance
was granted. Further, as also required by statute, the Board took into consideration the following five
issues in its balancing test:
1. Whether an undesirable change would be produced in the character of the neighborhood, or
a detriment to nearby properties would be created, by the granting of the requested area
variance.

The Village Code requires a minimum lot area of one (1) acre and a minimum side yard setback of

Attachment 3 - Schedule of Zoning District Regulations for the R-1A District.2
The Applicant proposes to construct a two-story 400 square-foot addition and a new 545+/-
square foot deck (12’ 1¾” x 44’ 10¾”) on an existing single-family dwelling located on a property
containing approximately 0.79-acres. The proposed addition will be twenty-one feet and a quarter inch
(21’ ¼”) from the northwestern property line. With the construction of the proposed addition and deck,
the Applicant requires the following two area variances:
1. A variance of 9,097 square feet (±0.21 acres) from the required minimum lot area of one (1) acre
(43,560 square feet) to allow a lot area of 34,463 square feet (±0.79 acres).
2. A variance of eight-feet and three-quarters of an inch (8’ ¾”) from the required minimum side yard
setback of thirty feet (30’) to allow a side yard setback of 21’ ¼” feet along the northwestern
property line.

DECISION
Village Code § 310-6(F): Combined Minimum Lot Area and Village Code § 310-7, Schedule of Zoning
District Regulations, HB District [Chapter 310 Attachment 7]) – Minimum Lot Area: Area Variance
Village Code § 310-6(F) provides that “[e]xcept as otherwise permitted (commercial center, industrial
park, etc.), where more than one principal use occupies a lot, said lot shall be large enough to satisfy the
minimum lot area or density requirements of each use.” On the HB Schedule of District Regulations, a
retail establishment must have a minimum lot area of 10,000 square feet, and dwelling units containing
no more than 2 bedrooms are required to have a minimum lot area of 6,000 square feet per unit. See
Village Code § 310-7 and Chapter 310 Attachment 7, Special Permit Use Nos. 8 & 15. The Applicant
proposes to maintain the existing commercial use, and convert the second story into two (2) dwelling
units. This requires a total minimum lot area of 22,000 square feet, and the property only consists of
21,209 square feet. Therefore, the Applicant is requesting a variance of 791 square feet.

fence in the front and side yards. Whereas pursuant to Section 146-5(B), no fence shall be more than
four (4) feet in height in any front or side yard. Said property is located in the R-2A Zoning District at 122
Pine Hill Road in Highland Mills and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 218, Block
2, Lot 44
DECISION
§ 146-5(B): Height Limitations: Area Variances
Village Code § 146-5(B) provides that “[n]o fence shall be more than four feet in height in any
front yard and side yard.” Village Code § 146-1 defines “front yard” as follows: “A ground area between
the street line (right-of-way) and a line drawn parallel or generally parallel thereto at the building line,
unoccupied by any building or structure other than exceptions as provided herein such as fences or
retaining walls. All corner properties adjacent to a public street, alley or highway shall also be considered
as a front yard for purposes of [Chapter 146]….” The Applicant’s property is located at the corner of Pine
Hill Drive and Skyline Drive and, thus, has front yards adjacent to both public streets.
The Applicant is requesting permission to construct a five (5) foot tall chain link fence in the
required front yards adjacent to Skyline Drive and Pine Hill Road, and in the required side yard located
perpendicular to Skyline Drive.
Consistent with its statutory obligations under New York State Village Law § 7-712-b when
considering an area variance, the Board balanced the benefit to the Applicant as weighed against the
detriment to the health, safety and welfare of the neighborhood or community if the requested variances
were granted. Further, as also required by statute, the Board took into consideration the following five
issues in its balancing test:
1. Whether an undesirable change would be produced in the character of the neighborhood, or
a detriment to nearby properties would be created,

and stated concerns for lot twelve and lot two. The Board asked for clarification on the inside lots in the

twelve are concerning. Chairman Brady stated Saltsman Creek runs into Woodbury creek which is where
most of the Village water comes from.

SEQRA. The Chairman also stated that the board is requesting confirmation on how the lots were
numbered in the previous application,

Attachment 11) provides that wall signs in the IB District are permitted to be a maximum of “10% of total

square feet, respectively, and thus appear to comply with the 10% limitation.

Review Decision for a variance for the construction of a parking area in front of a single-family
dwelling. Whereas pursuant to Section 310-7, the maximum permitted impervious surface coverage is
20%, and the Applicant is proposing 44.2%. Said property is in the R-1A Zoning District at 50 Greenwich
Avenue in Central Valley and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax Maps as Section 248 Block 1 Lot
44.
DECISION
Village Code § 310-7, Schedule of Zoning District Regulations, R-1A District [Chapter 310 Attachment 3])
– Maximum Lot Coverage: Area Variance
Pursuant to Village Code § 310-7, the Schedules of Zoning District Regulations are a part of the
Zoning Chapter and set forth the minimum or maximum zoning bulk and use requirements. The Schedule
of Zoning District Regulations for the R-1A District (Chapter 310, Attachment 3) permits a maximum lot
coverage 20%. The Applicant originally requested permission to construct a 3,079 square foot area of
pavers in the front yard of an existing single-family dwelling, which would result in a lot coverage of
46.1%.1 Therefore, the Applicant is requesting a variance of 26.1%.2 However, during the course of
proceedings before the Board, the Applicant revised its plan to remove 201 square feet of existing pavers
and convert the remaining 2,878 square feet to permeable pavers, which reduces the requested lot
coverage to 37.6% and reduces the size of the variance to 17.6%.
Consistent with its statutory obligations under New York State Village Law § 7-712-b when
considering an area variance, the Board balanced the benefit to the Applicant as weighed against the
detriment to the health, safety and welfare of the neighborhood or community if the requested variance
was granted. Further, as also required by statute, the Board took into consideration the following five
issues in its balancing test:
1 Although the Applicant has already constructed the area in the front yard, the Board considered the application as if
it were not constructed.

§ 146-7(A): Materials and Composition: Area Variance
Village Code § 146-5(B) provides that “[n]o fence shall be more than four feet in height in any
front yard and side yard.” Village Code § 146-7(A) provides that “no fence shall be erected in a front yard
in a residential district or along a public right-of-way unless the fence is uniformly less than 50% solid.”
The Applicant is requesting variances from these two provisions to permit the construction of a solid six
(6) foot tall wood panel fence in the front and side yards, and the property is located along the public
right-of-way of Ferndale Avenue.
Consistent with its statutory obligations under New York State Village Law § 7-712-b when
considering an area variance, the Board balanced the benefit to the Applicant as weighed against the
detriment to the health, safety and welfare of the neighborhood or community if the requested variances

Review decision of the application of Raizel Freund requesting variances for the construction of
a single-family dwelling. Whereas pursuant to Section 310-7, properties in the R-2A district are required
to have a minimum lot area of 2 acres, a minimum lot width of 175 feet, a minimum front yard setback
of 40 feet, a minimum side yard setback of 30 feet, a minimum setback for both side yards of 60 feet, a
minimum rear yard setback of 50 feet, and a maximum coverage of 20%. Said property is located in the
R-2A Zoning District at 7 Second Street in Highland Mills and is known on the Village of Woodbury Tax
Maps as Section 214, Block 8, Lot 1.
DECISION
Village Code § 310-43, Nonconforming Buildings: Discussion
Under Village Code § 310-43(B), “a building lawfully existing on July 11, 1990 (the day
immediately preceding the effective date of the Town predecessor to this chapter), or lawfully existing
on the day immediately preceding the effective date of any amendment to this chapter affecting such
building, regardless of change of title, possession or occupancy or right thereof, may be continued
indefinitely, subject to [certain identified restrictions].” The existing dwelling is a nonconforming
building as it does not conform to the dimensional requirements of the Zoning Code (i.e., lot area, lot
width, setbacks, and lot coverage.).1 The Applicant seeks to demolish the dwelling, and construct a
single-family dwelling in substantially the same location. The demolition of the existing structure
removes all rights the Applicant may have to any legally pre-existing nonconforming bulk and
dimensional conditions. Similarly, the abandonment of the nonconforming dwelling use removes all
rights the Applicant may have to any legally pre-existing nonconforming use. See Village Code § 310-
43.2.

Chairperson Hunter explained that this was to be a discussion of documents submitted for
proposed modifications to a recently approved wireless facility on an existing tower located on
Arden House Road. She then asked the applicant to introduce himself and give the Board a
brief overview.
John Furst, attorney for AT&T, introduced himself and said that he was here tonight to seek a
minor amendment to a previously approved Site Plan and Special Permit. He reminded the
Board that in September they had obtained conditional approval to extend the existing tower at
the end of Arden House Road by fifteen feet. At the top of this extended tower, AT&T was
going to locate panel antennas. After beginning construction, they realized that they would
need to add an additional dish antenna, which is approximately three feet in diameter and is
used to provide faster service to the facility. He explained that the Ethernet has to be beamed
in from another site a couple of miles away because they could not run lines underground.

expires in September.

Chairperson Hunter explained that this was to be a discussion of an application submitted for
proposed modification to an existing wireless facility for the removal and replacement of 2
antennas and associated equipment shelter at the base of the tower located on Arden House
Road. She then asked if there was anyone present for the applicant. At this point, Lauren
Catalano appeared on behalf of Sprint, seeking approval of the proposed modifications to the
tower at Arden House Road. She said that the modifications consist of swapping out two
antennas, adding one cabinet to the existing Nextel shelter, and adding three cables and four
remote radio heads. She also explained that the town’s wireless consultant had been sent the
construction drawings, as well as a report which states that they will be adding no additional
stress to the tower with the proposed modifications. Chairperson Hunter noted that the latest
set of plans she has is dated August 13, 2013. Ms. Catalano said that these were the correct
plans, as they have not changed.
Chairperson Hunter then asked M. Musso for his comments. M. Musso said that the plan set he
is working from is dated July 2, 2013. He added that, although the plans were submitted some
time ago, this is the first appearance by Sprint before the Planning Board. He went on to
explain that currently there are 12 panel antennas which are each four foot in length. Two of
these antennas will be changed out for newer technology antennas. These new antennas will
be six foot in length instead of four foot. The other antennas will remain unchanged. He said
that the other addition to the tower will be remote radio head units that will be mounted behind
the two new antennas. He said these are usually about 18” in length, and will be hidden fairly
well.

label: M
name: Musso also said that there will be a coordinated review of the structure of the tower, as

would be made within the shelter. He added that he is currently working on a review memo,
and the structural engineers are working on a structural review memo.
Chairperson Hunter asked D. Lindsay for his comments. D. Lindsay noted that the Board has a
standard condition regarding as-built drawings. He recommends that this condition be carried
through for this application. He also noted that a public hearing will be required for this
application. He also pointed out, as M. Musso had said, that the new equipment is a little taller
than the existing equipment. However, he felt this difference would be indistinguishable from
the ground. Also, he noted that the applicant is showing the tower as it currently is, without the
proposed 15’ addition. He said this should be discussed during any structural review.

Chairperson Hunter explained that this was to be a review of storm water documents submitted
for a proposed site plan and special permit to expand use to include a construction and
demolition recycling/processing center for property located at 911 Route 32 in Highland Mills.
Kevin Patton appeared as the engineer for the applicant and explained that he submitted a final
draft for the proposed storm water controls for the proposed concrete or C & D recycling facility.
He said the site currently has fairly limited storm water controls. By way of a review he said that
Route 32 runs along the east side of the property. The access road comes off Route 32 and
goes up the hill to the office and shop area. To the north is the quarry. The stone processing
plant and stockpiles are immediately south of the quarry, and the proposed C & D recycling
facility takes up about 1.6 acres immediately southwest of the office, extending from the base of
the railroad embankment out toward the slope that descends to Woodbury Creek. He showed
the Board a level area that is clear that is currently being used by Tomco in their pipeline
construction project. He said they have pipes and other materials stored there and they come in
and out on a daily basis loading and unloading materials.
Mr. Patton said there is an existing detention pond on the west side of the site that receives
runoff from the area of the proposed C & D facility and also some adjacent areas. This pond
has no outlet. It fills up with storm water in a major storm event and then overflows across the
gravel surface and pavement area to another small pond of the east side that has an outlet
control on it. This pond receives storm water from around the rock crusher and the scale area
and some of the other facility areas. The storm water controls and the existing SWPPP
describe that storm water will be filtered by a gravel berm where it discharges from the site. He
said it is very old and

proposed updated kiosk program to reflect recent architectural design for property located on
Route 32 in Central Valley. Jennifer Knarich of Price, Meese, Shulman and D’Arminio appeared
on behalf of the applicant, Simon Property Group who is the owner/operator of Woodbury
Common.
Woodbury Planning Bd. Mtg. 11 November 5, 2014
WOODBURY COMMON – TAX MAP 225-1-70.2 & 266-1-1 (cont.)
Ms. Knarich explained that they are here this evening with respect to a request for a formal
review to discuss the proposed kiosk program, specifically with respect to appearance and
design to reflect the recent architectural design changes. She said the kiosk locations were
approved in 2013 in the overall site plan on what she referred to as sheet C13. The Board had
received a submission packet. In this packet, Exhibit C shows the proposed locations for 8 units
out of the proposed 13 sites. She said the locations have not changed but some of the
proposed alternates have been swapped out. She then introduced John Villapiano who is
Director of Development for Simon, and Bill Pendergast who is overseeing the project.
Ms. Knarich then directed the Board’s attention to Exhibit A, which showed the existing kiosks.
These kiosks are outdated and, she said, are at a point where they cannot be updated or
rehabilitated. Exhibit B showed the proposed concept design for the updated kiosk program.
At this point John Villapiano addressed the Board. He said the RMU’s and kiosks within the
center have a long history of approvals. He said, as the Board is aware, the 2012 renovation
program is ongoing and it would be a missed opportunity not to update the kiosks to reflect and
match the rest of the center. He then referred to Exhibit A, which shows that the kiosk units are
significantly under-sized. If they are to upgrade the hardscape, landscape and building facades
and

label: D
name: Lindsay then wanted to address how the Board would proceed
raw_block:
could be done with a field change, such as the minor relocations. However, the size of the units
Woodbury Planning Bd. Mtg. 13 November 5, 2014
WOODBURY COMMON – TAX MAP 225-1-70.2 & 266-1-1 (cont.)
and the special permit conditions that relate to it and the hours of operation sound like it could
be an amended site plan. He said it is up to the Board to make a determination as to what they
will require. He felt that this would fall within the scope of the original SEQRA, so the Board
would just need to reaffirm SEQRA.
There was some discussion regarding how the Board should proceed. Mr. Pendergast said that
he sees it as a three step process. Step one is the approval of the 8 locations that were already
approved as a larger size, as long as the Board doesn’t require an amended site plan. Step two
is, can they be operated year round. And step three is making the other five alternates
permanent.
After some discussion all five Board members agreed that the 8 locations in a larger size could
be considered a field change. On the issue of the year round use, all five agreed that this could
be handled as a field change. D. Lindsay noted that this issue was a condition of the special
permit. He was not sure how to handle this. On the issue of making the five alternate locations
permanent, all five Board members said this could be handled as a field change. Chairperson
Hunter said the special permit conditions would need to be checked. Once this is done the
Board can make a final determination.
There was no further business for the Board to conduct, so Chairperson Hunter made a motion
to adjourn the meeting. R. Cataggio seconded the motion. The vote was as follows:

FLAUM MANAGEMENT/CAESARS ENTERTAINMENT - TAX MAP 243-1-4 thru
24
Chairperson Hunter explained that this was to be a discussion of balloon test protocol to be
performed for ARB of a proposed integrated resort, hotel and casino for property located on the
easterly side of NYS Route 17 adjacent to the train station. She noted that there were a lot of
guests in the audience and told them that this is not a public hearing, so no public comments
will be taken for either this application or the next application on the agenda.
At this point Steve Esposito, of Esposito Associates, appeared on behalf of the applicant and
explained that they were appearing tonight to discuss the protocols for the balloon test. He
noted that they were before the Board on September 17" at which time they presented
drawings for the proposed casino and hotel, as well as the site plan. At that time the Board had
requested the balloon test, which he said was not part of the scope that had been adopted for
this project. However, the client has agreed to perform the test.
Mr. Esposito then cited memos from both D. Lindsay’s office and S. Turner’s office and said that
he had prepared some responses to those letters and put together a scope for the balloon test
for the Board to review, as they would like to move forward as soon as possible. He then
distributed copies of his balloon test protocol to the Board members. Chairperson Hunter asked

label: R
name: Golden and D
raw_block:
Board.
Woodbury Planning Bd. Mtg. 5 November 19, 2014
AEON/HOTEL - TAX MAP 226.-1-21.2 (cont.)
Chairperson Hunter then asked D. Lindsay to continue with his comments. D. Lindsay said the
plans before the Board show the basic intent of the application. He said he gave the Board
some comments on the site plan and that one of those comments dealt with the problems of
trying to squeeze this density into a small space. The aisle-ways are not appropriate for 90
degree parking and need to be changed. He said they could do this with angled parking, and a
one-way circulation. He also said the site is narrow for moving fire equipment or any large
equipment, so they will need to make sure their turning radii are appropriate. This will also need
to be reviewed by the Fire Department.
With regard to drainage, D. Lindsay said there is only basic information included. A full SWPPP
will be needed for this application because they are disturbing more than one acre. The
applicant will also need to show how they will make connections to the sewer. Water will also
need to be addressed. Also, an architectural review will be required.

HIGHLAND SAND & GRAVEL — TAX MAP 203-1-8.21
Chairperson Hunter explained that this was to be a continuation of a Public Hearing for
proposed Site Plan and Special Permit to expand the use to include a construction and
demolition recycling/processing center on property located at 911 Route 32 in Highland Mills.
At this point Bob Krahulik, Attorney for the applicant, introduced himself as well as the Engineer
for the project Kevin Patton and Bill Derosher, President of Highland Sand and Gravel. He said
that they received a number of comments at last month’s meeting, and are in the process of
developing responses to those comments. A preliminary response was submitted to the Board
which primarily addressed some of the issues that have already been agreed to such as dust
mitigation, noise mitigation and the examination of the construction and demolition materials to
make sure they are not contaminated. He said he described various studies that have been
performed with respect to noise and also with respect to the Operations and Procedures manual
which will be implemented on the site. Mr. Krahulik also noted that as a condition to the Zoning
Board of Appeals approval, the applicant did agree to monitoring of air quality both on the site
and along properties that are adjacent to the quarry. He said they propose to have various
monitors directed on site and on a regular basis air quality will be tested. The equipment will
test the air quality over an 8 hour period to show air quality over the course of the day as
operations intensify or as operations are less intensive they will show the cycle of the air quality.
Mr. Krahulik said the Board had asked that a map be prepared that locates the residential
structures in relation to the quarry, as well as the commercial structures. Mr. Patton is working
on that map. By their next appearance before the Planning Board, the map will be ready and
will show the distance of the structures

Chairperson Hunter explained that this was to be a continuation of a public hearing for a
proposed amended site plan to include 30 additional parking spaces plus appurtenances with
an additional 37 storage units on property located at 27 Bond Street in Central Valley.
Jim Sweeney, attorney for the applicant, appeared and said that the applicant had addressed all
of the concerns which were brought up at the last meeting, except for one which he would
address later. He said they had addressed the lighting and the landscaping and the plan now
shows that the applicant agrees to enhance the strip of vegetation along the back of the
property, remove the dead vegetation, and keep this property maintained.
With regard to the shadow parking discussed at the last meeting, Mr. Sweeney said the
applicant continues to maintain that they do not need to show this parking. He said that some
kind of due process could be built into the resolution so that if it is needed it can be installed at a
later date. He added that this language is already in the draft resolution that was prepared.
Mr. Sweeney also recommended that something be added to the resolution of approval which
states that the total number of units permitted should be 430.

8 LAURA LANE LLC – TAX MAP 226-1-24.2
Chairperson Hunter explained that this was to be a review and discussion of a site plan
submitted for a proposed office and storage building on property located at 8 Laura Lane in
Central Valley. Stephen Greene appeared on behalf of the applicant and said that the property
in question is about four acres. It is a recycling center for cars and the owner wants to put a
building that is 60’ x 150’ on the property. The building will be 9000 square feet. He said there
was a little miscommunication as far as the height, and that the building will stay the same
height, but he wants to put an office inside which is two stories with a 3000 square foot office
space within the 9000 square foot building. He added that he showed the setbacks and the
current condition of the building. They have also proposed parking along the front of the
building along the road side. They plan to remove and modify the fence so nobody can get to
the back of the building during non-operational hours. He said the only problem they have right
now is the drainage. He said the drain is okay, but it is currently clogged.
Chairperson Hunter asked where this site is located. Mr. Greene said it is the second driveway
on the right after going under the railroad trestle. The recycling yard has a big red fence around
it. Chairperson Hunter asked if Laura Lane was a private road or a Town road. Mr. Greene
said it was a private road but it is plowed by the Highway Department. D. Lindsay said that the
applicant owns the road bed. D. Lindsay will check with the Highway Superintendent on this.
Mr. Greene said that on the plan he set the building back 50’ from what would be the assumed
right-of-way if the road was a 50’ road so there would not be any conflict in the
geo_display_name: 8, Laura Lane, Central Valley, Village of Woodbury, Town of Woodbury, Orange County, New York, 10917, United States

land occupied by such use. Nor shall any evidence of such use be increased by any means

conforming uses, but it gives that authority to the Zoning Board of Appeals. With this knowledge
he said the only thing the Board could do is refer this to the ZBA.

MELODY LANE PROPERTY LLC – TAX MAP 225-1-36
Chairperson Hunter explained that this was to be a review and discussion of a site plan
submitted for a proposed auto dealership with accessory offices, service bays and parking on
vacant property located on Melody Lane. George Lithco appeared as counsel for the applicant
and explained that they were there to give the Board an overview of a proposed Mercedes Benz
dealership. He said with him was Jake Resta from Mercedes Benz Orange County who will
give the Board some background. Project architect Kevin Spink will give the Board the basic
layout of the building and the house model that is being proposed. Also with him is Andrew
Fetherston and Pete Russillo of Maser Consulting. Andrew will talk about the site plan and Pete
will talk about the access. He then introduced Jake Resta.
Mr. Resta explained that they have a number of dealerships in New Jersey, and that this is not a
new business for them but something they have been doing for quite some time. They currently
operate a Mercedes Benz and Audi dealership in New Jersey and they are the fifth largest
Mercedes Benz dealer in the country. Over the summer they were approached by Mercedes
Benz to present a business plan for Orange County, with the emphasis on locating in
Middletown. He said they felt that this community was a better community for them, so the
business plan was incorporated here. He said it is related much more closely to the way they
operate, and the community feel was a better overall environment as well. He said that both of
their stores in New Jersey are in heavy residential neighborhoods. They are well aware of
some of the complications that come with car dealerships on local residents and they will try to
mitigate this as much as they possibly can.
Mr. Resta said they operate a very “green” operation. Ninety percent of all materials in their
operation are recycled. All of the fluids are captured and recycled and approximately 5% or so
of the car wash wa

at that time. However, with the materials they have now, he would not suggest a public hearing
at this time. C. Scibelli suggested that the applicant hold their own hearing, like a Town Hall
meeting. R. Golden did not think this was a good idea, as the Board would not hear the public’s
input. He said the whole purpose of a public hearing on any application is so that the Board
hears the public and can take the public’s comments into consideration in their determination of
how they plan to proceed. He added that some people may feel that they’ve gotten their
information and not attend any public hearing that the Board may have. He said he was not
saying this can’t be done but weighing in on his opinion.

Chairperson Hunter explained that this was to be a continuation of a public hearing for proposed
site plan and special permit to expand the use to include a construction and demolition
recycling/processing center. The property is located at 911 Route 32 in Highland Mills, within
the R2A zoning district. She then listed the following letters which she had received: A letter
from Victor Ferrarelli, the Chairman of the Village of Woodbury Zoning Board of Appeals, a letter
from the Town of Cornwall Supervisor, a letter and notes from the Moodna Creek Watershed
Inter-municipal Council signed by Michael Sweeten, a letter was received from Hydroquest
dated February 10th, with a copy of an e-mail with corrected language. She asked that the
Board members only address the copy that was placed in their mailboxes, dated February 18th.
A second packet of information was received from Mary Gross-Ferraro today, as well as
correspondence from Kevin Patton dated February 11th and received on February 17th with his
response to Hydroquest. Also, there is a fax copy from the Orange County Department of
Planning that was received on the 18th from Fred Budde.
Chairperson Hunter also noted that the applicant had submitted a map in response to her
request, which shows the location of the homes along Route 32. A letter dated February 12th
was received from Bob Krahulik, and D. Lindsay submitted his report dated February 13th.
Chairperson Hunter then informed the public that someone has been handing out minutes that
are not Village of Woodbury minutes.
At this point R. Cataggio asked who Fred Budde was. Chairperson Hunter said that he works in
David Church’s office. R. Cataggio just wanted this noted for the record.
Woodbury Planning Bd. Mtg. 2 February 18, 2015

that are entering the creek provide the water

name: Lindsay said that the applicant proposes to process inert materials, which are clean materials

Woodbury Planning Bd. Mtg. 4 February 18, 2015
HIGHLAND SAND & GRAVEL – TAX MAP 203-1-8.21 (cont.)
Chairperson Hunter asked S. Turner for his comments. He said that the Board may very well
want to revisit SEQRA, as K. Naughton had suggested. The letter from the County indicated
that SEQRA had never been done. S. Turner wanted the record to show that SEQRA was done
prior to the Zoning Board’s decision with the information that was available to the Board at that
time. Chairperson Hunter said that R. Golden is working on a letter to the County to address
some erroneous information that was in their letter. K. Naughton said that they did speak to
Fred Budde today and that there are a number of inaccuracies in that letter. The first one is
regarding SEQRA. Also, he refers to the Village Comprehensive Plan, which has been
rendered null and void. There are also other inaccuracies. She said they will be preparing a
response to advise them of these inaccuracies so he is well aware that the Board has taken the
proper action.
At this point Chairperson Hunter wanted clarification on the items that will be accepted at the
C & D site. These items are: asphalt, concrete, brick, masonry, soil and rock. She asked if
there would be debris from buildings being torn down. Mr. Patton said there could be debris
from buildings, but the accepted materials would not include materials like wallboard or floor tile.
The materials have to be recognizable materials that fit the categories listed by the Chairperson.
Chairperson Hunter also noted that the materials would need to come from a commercial
hauler. Individuals could not bring mate

label: K
name: Naughton to explain the process
raw_block:
law that Mr. Siebold is referencing. However, if SEQRA is reopened the Board does not have
jurisdiction to take any action at this time. If the Board does rescind the Negative Declaration
and reopen the SEQRA process, they wouldn’t have to take action until SEQRA was closed.
Also, if the Board closes the public hearing they would ask the applicant to waive the 62 day
time frame, even though they wouldn’t be able to take action.
Mr. Siebold said that he thinks the Board has the best interests of the Town at heart and that
their wisdom will prevail. He said they will look at the big picture and see the potential for
positives and negatives. He then cited the final paragraph of the letter from David Church’s
office regarding impacts to Woodbury Creek. He said that there is nothing more important to
the community than water, and if we don’t have water we are in trouble.
Bob Till, 46 year resident of Woodbury, Former Building Inspector for Woodbury, Assistant to
the Tax Assessor for Woodbury and five term Town Supervisor introduced himself and said he
wanted to give the Board some history of the C & D law that was adopted. He said that in April
of 1988 the Tuxedo Landfill was a “fiasco”. He said when they were closing it he was getting
calls from contractors to fill every hole in the Town. He immediately notified the Board that
something needed to be done because the DEC did not protect us, as they had no jurisdiction
over the Tuxedo Landfill. With regard to C & D material, he said he was a firefighter in the City
of New York and many times they were called to a construction site that was on fire or a
demolition site because they were cutting with torches. He said when they were loading stuff
onto trucks everything went. Mr. Till said there is no way to ever monitor what comes into this
site.
Mr. Till also said that he was not sure how this application came to the Planning Board because
the use is illegal. He said the Zoning Board can’t approve anything that is illegal.

MOSLEM – TAX MAP 248-1-68, 69 & 70 (formerly 218-2-85.21)
Chairperson Hunter explained that this was to be a continuation of a public hearing for
amendments to a previously approved subdivision site plan to include the removal of a carriage
house located on lot 3, and relocation of a single family dwelling on lot 1. The property is
located on Smith Clove Road in Central Valley.
Chairperson Hunter then noted that the Board did not do a site visit because they did not
receive revised plans. She said that during discussions with D. Lindsay she found out that there
were six items that were requested by the Board. Four of them were not done, and two of them
were unclear. She added that the intent was to allow the Planning Board to understand current
site conditions and compare impacts with the previously approved plan. All revisions were to be
shown in an overlay fashion to allow easy comparison, however, the Board did not receive this.
Stephen Greene, engineer for the applicant, explained that some of the items were put on the
plan and some of the others were not practical to do. The dirt that is piled in front of the
property was frozen so could not be removed. Also he said that with respect to the trees, he did
show what was remaining from what was proposed originally. He did not show the house and
the proposed driveway. Chairperson Hunter then listed the things that the Board has asked for
such as the overlay of the new and previously approved building lots, the clearing limits for the
previously approved construction, required clearing limits for the proposed construction and
existing clearing. She then said that she would allow any members of the public to speak,
however, she did not feel that the Board had the information that was required to continue this
public hearing. D. Lindsay noted that the applicant would need to submit their information by
March 18th in order to be listed on the April 1st agenda.

Mr. Pitingaro explained that this was to be a 124 room hotel on approximately 11 acres
bordering the Thruway to the East and Woodbury Creek to the west. He said he would be
happy to discuss the consultant’s comments or any comments the Board has on the submitted
materials.
Chairperson Hunter then asked D. Lindsay for his comments. D. Lindsay said that the applicant
has submitted a revised plan and included some details. He said that one of the principle items
shown on the plan concerns zoning. The Board had received an opinion from G. Thomasberger
that the driveway that is proposed that circles the building and runs parallel to Estrada Road can
be installed and is not in violation of the code. However, they have a portico that is one of their
main focal points. They provided architectural renderings that show this as a typical entrance
that is in front of a hotel where there is a large covered area that goes into the reception area.
This is not permitted in the front yard area. Because the plan is not in compliance with the
zoning it would need to go to the ZBA for review. D. Lindsay noted that the applicant’s
correspondent said they would apply at the appropriate time, and this is the appropriate time.

hundred years and they currently operate three dealerships in Bergen County, New Jersey. He
said that Mercedes Benz was looking to expand and have a dealership in Orange County and
originally wanted them to explore operating in Middletown. He said he felt that this would be a
better community overall that fits more in the way they operate in Bergen County and the way
they like to operate. Mercedes finally did accept their business case and gave them the
opportunity to move forward. They are currently the sixth largest Mercedes Benz dealer in the
country and have a long track record of success. Outside of their sales success they also
continue to win customer experience awards. In 2014 they also won the employee choice
award for best dealership to work for. Mr. Agresta said they operate in a residential community
in Bergen County and realize that a car dealership can have impact on the neighbors. They
want to make sure they minimize this impact as much as they possibly can. He added that

the ZBA conditioned it upon approvals from the Planning Board, compliance with site plan
engineering approvals, and any conditions imposed by the Architectural Review Board.
With regard to the buffer, Mr. May said the code requires that a building fronting on a State
Highway or County road have the front yard unoccupied and open to the sky for a distance of
not less than fifty feet. He said they comply with this requirement. The design criteria for the
CR district requires off street parking to be provided in the rear and on one side behind the front
building line. They also comply with this requirement.
Mr. May said the Village Engineer also brought up the performance standards, which would be
atmospheric effluence, glare, heat and industrial waste. He said this project will not have any
problem with these performance standards.

proposed wetlands mitigation. He explained that a rather extensive boundary and topographic
survey has been prepared which included a parcel that was owned by the New York State DOT.

showed the Board the location of the wetlands, and the portion they will be filling in. He said the
total wetland fill is .47 acres. He then showed the Board the location of a drain pipe that carries

property through a ditch. There is also a high ground area which

Chairperson Hunter explained that this was to be a discussion of a site plan submitted for a
proposed office and storage building on property located at 8 Laura Lane in Central Valley. It
was also noted that the ZBA decision permitted the use saying that an expansion of 10% of the
floor area can be permitted. This expansion was determined to be only 8.2%, so the request
was granted.
Stephen Greene appeared as the engineer for the project. He said the building that was
displayed for the Board is 144’ x 60’, with 3,000 square feet of interior office space. The
remaining portion will be storage space for parts that are recycled off of the cars. There will be
no storage of fuel, and there is no waste going into the building.
Mr. Greene said the outside of the building will be illuminated by wall pack units. These will be
on during the hours of operation for the building, and will go off around 6:00 p.m. There is a
security light in front of the building. The building will also be serviced by water and sewer
which is located in the street.
Chairperson Hunter asked about the proposed sign. Mr. Greene said it would be a single faced
sign because they are on a dead end street. This sign will be lit by three gooseneck lights
mounted on the sign. The sign will be constructed of either a composite material or aluminum
and will be a stone based monument sign, and will be located right before the first entrance into
the building.
Chairperson Hunter then asked D. Lindsay for his comments. He said that the ZBA granted the
expanded use, but the decision was silent on area variances for yard setbacks. He said he had
noted in his report to the ZBA that since this is not a permitted use in the zone there are no
designations for setbacks for yard areas. He recommended that the Board consult with

transcript. The applicant’s attorney said they would. She then asked the applicant to state his

Joseph Scarmatto appeared as the attorney for the owner of Ace Farm, Amazon Realty
Associates LLC. He said the owner is proposing a re-grading of the site and planting of
wetlands materials to expand the existing wetlands on the site. He explained that the applicant
has an affiliation with another entity that happens to be in the Village of Kiryas Joel. Some
construction occurred there and some wetlands that were regulated by the Army Corps of
Engineers were impacted. The Army Corps asked them to consider the creation of some
wetlands on other lands controlled by the entity or its affiliates as a mitigation. This particular
owner is affiliated and was willing, because they had a site where there are some existing
wetlands. He said the Army Corps visited the site and flagged the existing wetlands, as they
saw an opportunity to expand those wetlands to satisfy the requirements of the Corps with
Woodbury Planning Bd. Mtg. 7 July 1, 2015,

before the Planning Board. He said he talked to the Building Inspector and he said that Section
310-45c1 says, “a site plan is required and must be submitted for any application that makes
use of vacant land or has a change of use or intensity of use which affects drainage.” He said
this is what the Building Inspector worked under in terms of whether they needed an application.

site plan, and also requires a public hearing, as it is either a change in use of the vacant land
and/or a change in the intensity of use that affects drainage. He also said that, given the

With respect to SEQRA, R. Golden cited a letter dated April 2015 from the Army Corps of
Engineers. It is addressed to the United Talmudical Academy in Kiryas Joel and indicates that it
is being ordered to carry out this work to resolve “this enforcement matter”. He added that there
is a Type Two action of SEQRA that says if the work is something that is being carried out as
part of an enforcement order it is a Type Two action. However, the problem here is that the
United Talmudical Academy of Kiryas Joel is not the applicant. If it was the applicant then, in
his opinion, this would be a Type Two action. Right now there is just Simon Gruber who is
apparently acting on his own and not in accordance with the Army Corps of Engineers.
Therefore, it would not be a Type Two action. He said that the Planning Board would have to
type the action and make a determination of significance under SEQRA.
Chairperson Hunter asked the Board members if they had any questions or comments.

name: Kestenbaum then wanted to ask some questions regarding the current hotel application
raw_block:
explained for F. Doneit, who was not present at the public hearing, that a question was brought
up during the hearing as to whether the Negative Declaration under SEQRA should be revisited
in light of some of the public comments. He then cited some portions of Section 617.7 in the
SEQRA regulations on Criteria for Determining Significance, particularly a section dealing with
“existing community or neighborhood character”. He said the residents brought up the existing
community and neighborhood character in the area of the hotel and said that this is a significant
change that would have significant impact. G. Kestenbaum also cited “the creation of a hazard
to human health”. He said it may not be human health but it certainly is human safety because
they were concerned about the character of the traffic. He said the quantity of traffic may not
change significantly, but the quality of the traffic will. There will be people who are not familiar
with the area driving on the roads who may not know that there are students waiting for buses
with no kiosks, and no sidewalks. He was also concerned because the traffic consultant said
she did not do a qualitative analysis of the traffic because it was not requested by the Board.
He wondered if the Board could request such an analysis in the future.

label: R
name: Golden pointed out that the list that G
raw_block:
considerations for every application to make a Positive or Negative Declaration. He said the
SEQRA regulations require that Boards make a determination of significance as early on in the
process as possible. This may mean that the Board may not have all the information it needs. If
new information comes to light, such as the information gathered at the public hearing, the
Board is allowed to revisit the Negative Declaration. If a Positive Declaration is issued, then the
Board needs to decide whether they will require an Environmental Impact Statement or an
expanded EAF, which he said would be the shorter process. R. Golden further added that if the
traffic engineer says, from their professional experience, they do not see an adverse
environmental impact it would be difficult for the Board to say they disagree because the courts
will say that they don’t have the same expertise. He said the Board would need to be very
careful going against one of their own consultants. He also said that G. Kestenbaum brought up
a lot of good points with respect to why the Board may want to reconsider the initial Negative
Declaration. He said the Board has to consider all of those things, and should identify any
particular items of significance that they now believe may have a significant adverse
environmental impact and make a determination as to whether or not they want to change the
Negative Declaration to a Positive Declaration. He said that as long as this is done on a rational
basis, the Board has the right to do it.
